([/ˈædɪleɪd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-au-Adelaide.oga "File:En-au-Adelaide.oga") [*AD-il-ayd*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Kaurna](/wiki/Kaurna_language "Kaurna language"): *Tarndanya* [[ˈd̪̥aɳɖaɲa]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")) is the [capital](/wiki/List_of_Australian_capital_cities "List of Australian capital cities") and most populous city of [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia"), as well as the [fifth-most populous city in Australia](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Australia_by_population "List of cities in Australia by population"). The name "Adelaide" may refer to either **Greater Adelaide** (including the [Adelaide Hills](/wiki/Adelaide_Hills "Adelaide Hills")) or the [Adelaide city centre](/wiki/Adelaide_city_centre "Adelaide city centre"); the [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") *Adelaidean* is used to denote the city and the residents of Adelaide. The [traditional owners](/wiki/Native_title_in_Australia#Traditional_owner "Native title in Australia") of the Adelaide region are the [Kaurna](/wiki/Kaurna "Kaurna"),[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) with the name *Tarndanya* referring to the area of the city centre and surrounding [Park Lands](/wiki/Adelaide_Park_Lands "Adelaide Park Lands"), in the [Kaurna language](/wiki/Kaurna_language "Kaurna language").[[14]](#cite_note-14) Adelaide is situated on the [Adelaide Plains](/wiki/Adelaide_Plains "Adelaide Plains") north of the [Fleurieu Peninsula](/wiki/Fleurieu_Peninsula "Fleurieu Peninsula"), between the [Gulf St Vincent](/wiki/Gulf_St_Vincent "Gulf St Vincent") in the west and the [Mount Lofty Ranges](/wiki/Mount_Lofty_Ranges "Mount Lofty Ranges") in the east. Its metropolitan area encompasses over [430 suburbs](/wiki/List_of_Adelaide_suburbs "List of Adelaide suburbs"), extending 96 km (60 mi) from [Gawler](/wiki/Gawler "Gawler") in the north to [Sellicks Beach](/wiki/Sellicks_Beach "Sellicks Beach") in the south and 20 km (12 mi) from the western coast to the eastern [foothills](/wiki/Adelaide_Hills "Adelaide Hills") of the Mount Lofty Ranges.

**Adelaide** ([/ˈædɪleɪd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-au-Adelaide.oga "File:En-au-Adelaide.oga") [*AD-il-ayd*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key");[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Kaurna](/wiki/Kaurna_language "Kaurna language"): *Tarndanya* [[ˈd̪̥aɳɖaɲa]](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA")) is the [capital](/wiki/List_of_Australian_capital_cities "List of Australian capital cities") and most populous city of [South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia "South Australia"), as well as the [fifth-most populous city in Australia](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Australia_by_population "List of cities in Australia by population"). The name "Adelaide" may refer to either **Greater Adelaide** (including the [Adelaide Hills](/wiki/Adelaide_Hills "Adelaide Hills")) or the [Adelaide city centre](/wiki/Adelaide_city_centre "Adelaide city centre"); the [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") *Adelaidean* is used to denote the city and the residents of Adelaide. The [traditional owners](/wiki/Native_title_in_Australia#Traditional_owner "Native title in Australia") of the Adelaide region are the [Kaurna](/wiki/Kaurna "Kaurna"),[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) with the name *Tarndanya* referring to the area of the city centre and surrounding [Park Lands](/wiki/Adelaide_Park_Lands "Adelaide Park Lands"), in the [Kaurna language](/wiki/Kaurna_language "Kaurna language").[[14]](#cite_note-14) Adelaide is situated on the [Adelaide Plains](/wiki/Adelaide_Plains "Adelaide Plains") north of the [Fleurieu Peninsula](/wiki/Fleurieu_Peninsula "Fleurieu Peninsula"), between the [Gulf St Vincent](/wiki/Gulf_St_Vincent "Gulf St Vincent") in the west and the [Mount Lofty Ranges](/wiki/Mount_Lofty_Ranges "Mount Lofty Ranges") in the east. Its metropolitan area encompasses over [430 suburbs](/wiki/List_of_Adelaide_suburbs "List of Adelaide suburbs"), extending 96 km (60 mi) from [Gawler](/wiki/Gawler "Gawler") in the north to [Sellicks Beach](/wiki/Sellicks_Beach "Sellicks Beach") in the south and 20 km (12 mi) from the western coast to the eastern [foothills](/wiki/Adelaide_Hills "Adelaide Hills") of the Mount Lofty Ranges.

Named in honour of [Adelaide of Saxe-Meiningen](/wiki/Adelaide_of_Saxe-Meiningen "Adelaide of Saxe-Meiningen"), wife of [King William IV](/wiki/William_IV "William IV"), the city was founded in 1836 as the [planned capital](/wiki/New_town "New town") for the only freely settled British province in Australia, distinguishing it from Australia's [penal colonies](/wiki/Penal_colony "Penal colony").[[15]](#cite_note-15) [Colonel William Light](/wiki/William_Light "William Light"), one of Adelaide's founding fathers, designed the city centre and chose its location close to the [River Torrens](/wiki/River_Torrens "River Torrens"). Light's design, now [listed as national heritage](/wiki/Australian_National_Heritage_List "Australian National Heritage List"), set out the city centre in a [grid layout](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan") known as "[Light's Vision](/wiki/Light%27s_Vision "Light's Vision")", interspaced by wide boulevards and large public squares, and entirely surrounded by park lands. Colonial Adelaide was noted for its leading examples of religious freedom and progressive political reforms and became known as the "[City of Churches](/wiki/City_of_Churches "City of Churches")" due to its diversity of faiths. It was Australia's third-most populous city until the [postwar](/wiki/Postwar "Postwar") era.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Today, Adelaide is one of Australia's most visited [travel destinations](/wiki/Tourism_in_Australia "Tourism in Australia")[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) and hosts [many festivals and sporting events](/wiki/Category:Organised_events_in_Adelaide "Category:Organised events in Adelaide"), such as the [Adelaide 500](/wiki/Adelaide_500 "Adelaide 500"), [Tour Down Under](/wiki/Tour_Down_Under "Tour Down Under"), [Gather Round](/wiki/Gather_Round "Gather Round"), [LIV Golf Adelaide](/wiki/LIV_Golf_Adelaide "LIV Golf Adelaide"), and the [Adelaide Fringe](/wiki/Adelaide_Fringe "Adelaide Fringe"), the world's second largest annual arts festival. Adelaide also hosts men's tennis with an [ATP 250](/wiki/ATP_250_tournaments "ATP 250 tournaments") event and women's tennis with a [WTA 500](/wiki/WTA_500_tournaments "WTA 500 tournaments") event, and will also become the new host of the [Australian MotoGP Grand Prix](/wiki/Australian_motorcycle_Grand_Prix "Australian motorcycle Grand Prix") from 2027.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The city has also been renowned for its [automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Australia#Holden "Automotive industry in Australia"), having been the original host of the [Australian Grand Prix](/wiki/Australian_Grand_Prix "Australian Grand Prix") in the [FIA Formula One World Championship](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") from 1985 to 1995. Other features include its [food and wine](/wiki/South_Australian_wine "South Australian wine") industries, its coastline and hills, its large defence and manufacturing operations, and its emerging space sector, including the [Australian Space Agency](/wiki/Australian_Space_Agency "Australian Space Agency") being headquartered there. With [one of the world's largest foreign-born populations](/wiki/Foreign_born#Cities_with_largest_foreign_born_populations "Foreign born"), Adelaide has consistently ranked within the [top-ten most liveable cities globally](/wiki/World%27s_most_liveable_cities "World's most liveable cities") for much of the 21st century, being named in 2021 the most liveable city in the country and third in the world.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Its aesthetic appeal has also been recognised by *[Architectural Digest](/wiki/Architectural_Digest "Architectural Digest")*, which ranked Adelaide as the most beautiful city in the world in 2024.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

As South Australia's government and commercial centre, Adelaide is the site of many governmental and financial institutions. Most of these are concentrated in the [central business district](/wiki/Adelaide_central_business_district "Adelaide central business district") along the cultural boulevards of [North Terrace](/wiki/North_Terrace,_Adelaide "North Terrace, Adelaide") and [King William Street](/wiki/King_William_Street,_Adelaide "King William Street, Adelaide"). Adelaide has also been classed as a [Gamma + level](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network#Gamma_+ "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city") as categorised by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network"), with the city further linking economic regions to the worldwide economy.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Adelaide is connected by extensive [bus, train and tram networks](/wiki/Transport_in_Adelaide "Transport in Adelaide"), all of which are operated by [Adelaide Metro](/wiki/Adelaide_Metro "Adelaide Metro"), with its main railway terminus at the [Adelaide railway station](/wiki/Adelaide_railway_station "Adelaide railway station"). The city is also served by [Adelaide Airport](/wiki/Adelaide_Airport "Adelaide Airport") and [Port Adelaide](/wiki/Port_Adelaide "Port Adelaide"), both of which are among the busiest airports and seaports in Australia, respectively.

## History

Main articles: [History of Adelaide](/wiki/History_of_Adelaide "History of Adelaide") and [Timeline of Adelaide history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Adelaide_history "Timeline of Adelaide history")

### Before European settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Before European settlement")]

[![Area to the east of Gulf St Vincent highlighted](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Kaurnaland.png/250px-Kaurnaland.png)](/wiki/File:Kaurnaland.png)

The approximate extent of Kaurna territory, based on the description by Amery (2000)

The amount of time Aboriginal people have been living in the area around modern-day Adelaide has been described as "millennia",[[23]](#cite_note-23) "tens of thousands of years",[[24]](#cite_note-24) "thousands of generations",[[25]](#cite_note-25) or "innumerable generations".[[26]](#cite_note-26) At [Hallett Cove Conservation Park](/wiki/Hallett_Cove_Conservation_Park "Hallett Cove Conservation Park") in Adelaide's southern suburbs, archaeological evidence of Aboriginal culture has been found dating back 40,000 years.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

The area was originally inhabited by the [Kaurna](/wiki/Kaurna "Kaurna") people, one of many [Aboriginal](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australian "Aboriginal Australian") tribes in South Australia. The city and [parklands](/wiki/Adelaide_park_lands "Adelaide park lands") area also known as *Tarndanyangga*[[note 1]](#cite_note-31) *Tandanya*[[note 2]](#cite_note-32), *Tarndanya*[[31]](#cite_note-33) or *Tarntanya*.[[32]](#cite_note-34) The name means 'male red kangaroo rock', referring to a rock formation on the site that has now been destroyed.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

The surrounding area was an open, grassy plain with patches of trees and shrubs, which had been managed by hundreds of generations. Kaurna country encompassed the plains stretching north and south of Tarntanya, as well as the wooded foothills of the [Mt Lofty Ranges](/wiki/Mount_Lofty_Ranges "Mount Lofty Ranges"). The River Torrens was known as the Karrawirra Pari (Red Gum forest river). About 300 Kaurna populated the Adelaide area,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] and were referred to by the settlers as the Cowandilla.[[34]](#cite_note-KaurnaSA-36)

The more than 20 local clans across the plain lived seminomadic lives, with extensive [mound settlements](/wiki/Mound "Mound") where huts were built repeatedly over centuries and a complex social structure, including a class of sorcerers separated from regular society.[[35]](#cite_note-37)

Within a few decades of [European settlement of South Australia](/wiki/European_settlement_of_South_Australia "European settlement of South Australia"), Kaurna culture was almost completely lost, with the last speaker of [Kaurna language](/wiki/Kaurna_language "Kaurna language") having died in 1929.[[36]](#cite_note-38) Extensive documentation by early missionaries and other researchers has enabled a modern revival of both,[[37]](#cite_note-Amery-39) which has included a commitment by local and state governments to rename or include Kaurna names for many local places.[[38]](#cite_note-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![Painting of person](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Beechey%2C_William_-_Adelaide_of_Saxe-Meiningen_-_NPG_1533.jpg/250px-Beechey%2C_William_-_Adelaide_of_Saxe-Meiningen_-_NPG_1533.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beechey,_William_-_Adelaide_of_Saxe-Meiningen_-_NPG_1533.jpg)

[Queen Adelaide](/wiki/Adelaide_of_Saxe-Meiningen "Adelaide of Saxe-Meiningen"), after whom the city was named

[![Refer to caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Adelaide_supplement_to_the_Illustrated_Sydney_News.png/250px-Adelaide_supplement_to_the_Illustrated_Sydney_News.png)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_supplement_to_the_Illustrated_Sydney_News.png)

In July 1876, the *Illustrated Sydney News* published a special supplement that included an early aerial view of the City of Adelaide: (South) Adelaide (the CBD), River Torrens, and portion of North Adelaide from a point above Strangways Terrace, [North Adelaide](/wiki/North_Adelaide "North Adelaide")

Based on the ideas of [Edward Gibbon Wakefield](/wiki/Edward_Gibbon_Wakefield "Edward Gibbon Wakefield") about colonial reform, [Robert Gouger](/wiki/Robert_Gouger "Robert Gouger") petitioned the British government to create a new colony in Australia, resulting in the passage of the [South Australia Act 1834](/wiki/South_Australia_Act_1834 "South Australia Act 1834"). Physical establishment of the colony began with the arrival of the first British colonisers in February 1836, followed by the commencement of colonial government in South Australia on 28 December 1836. Proclaimed by the [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_South_Australia "Governor of South Australia") near [The Old Gum Tree](/wiki/The_Old_Gum_Tree "The Old Gum Tree") in what is now the suburb of [Glenelg North](/wiki/Glenelg_North "Glenelg North"), this day is annually commemorated in South Australia as [Proclamation Day](/wiki/Proclamation_Day_(South_Australia) "Proclamation Day (South Australia)").[[40]](#cite_note-42) Named for [Queen Adelaide](/wiki/Queen_Adelaide "Queen Adelaide"), the site of the colony's capital was surveyed and laid out by Colonel [William Light](/wiki/William_Light "William Light"), the first surveyor-general of South Australia, with his own original, unique and topographically sensitive design.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

Adelaide was established as a planned colony of free immigrants, promising civil liberties and freedom from religious persecution, based upon the ideas of [Edward Gibbon Wakefield](/wiki/Edward_Gibbon_Wakefield "Edward Gibbon Wakefield"). Wakefield had read accounts of Australian settlement while in prison in London for attempting to abduct an heiress,[[42]](#cite_note-44) and realised that the eastern colonies suffered from a lack of available labour, due to the practice of giving land grants to all arrivals.[[43]](#cite_note-45) Wakefield's idea was for the Government to survey and sell the land at a rate that would maintain land values high enough to be unaffordable for labourers and journeymen.[[44]](#cite_note-46) Funds raised from the sale of land were to be used to bring out working-class emigrants, who would have to work hard for the monied settlers to ever afford their own land.[[45]](#cite_note-47) As a result of this policy, Adelaide does not share [the convict settlement history](/wiki/Convictism_in_Australia "Convictism in Australia") of other Australian cities like [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") and [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart").

[![Painting of a town near a river with woodlands and hills in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/North_Terrace%2C_1841.jpg/250px-North_Terrace%2C_1841.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_Terrace,_1841.jpg)

[North Terrace](/wiki/North_Terrace,_Adelaide "North Terrace, Adelaide") in 1841

As it was believed that in a colony of free settlers there would be little crime, no provision was made for a [gaol](/wiki/Prison "Prison") in Colonel Light's 1837 plan. But by mid-1837 the *[South Australian Register](/wiki/South_Australian_Register "South Australian Register")* was warning of escaped convicts from New South Wales and tenders for a temporary gaol were sought. Following a burglary, a murder, and two attempted murders in Adelaide during March 1838, Governor Hindmarsh created the South Australian Police Force (now the [South Australia Police](/wiki/South_Australia_Police "South Australia Police")) in April 1838 under 21-year-old [Henry Inman](/wiki/Henry_Inman_(police_commander) "Henry Inman (police commander)").[[46]](#cite_note-48) The first sheriff, Samuel Smart, was wounded during a robbery, and on 2 May 1838 one of the offenders, Michael Magee, became the first person to be hanged in South Australia.[[47]](#cite_note-49) William Baker Ashton was appointed governor of the temporary gaol in 1839, and in 1840 George Strickland Kingston was commissioned to design Adelaide's new gaol.[[48]](#cite_note-50) Construction of [Adelaide Gaol](/wiki/Adelaide_Gaol "Adelaide Gaol") commenced in 1841.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

Adelaide's early history was marked by economic uncertainty and questionable leadership.[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Adelaide#Dubious "Talk:Adelaide")*] The first governor of South Australia, [John Hindmarsh](/wiki/John_Hindmarsh "John Hindmarsh"), clashed frequently with others, in particular the Resident Commissioner, [James Hurtle Fisher](/wiki/James_Hurtle_Fisher "James Hurtle Fisher"). The rural area surrounding Adelaide was surveyed by Light in preparation to sell a total of over 405 km2 (156 sq mi) of land. Adelaide's early economy started to get on its feet in 1838 with the arrival of livestock from [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Australia "Victoria, Australia"), [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") and [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania"). Wool production provided an early basis for the South Australian economy. By 1860, wheat farms had been established from [Encounter Bay](/wiki/Encounter_Bay "Encounter Bay") in the south to [Clare](/wiki/Clare,_South_Australia "Clare, South Australia") in the north.

[![Refer to caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Adelaide_MKL_Bd._1_1890_%28133265370%29.jpg/250px-Adelaide_MKL_Bd._1_1890_%28133265370%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_MKL_Bd._1_1890_(133265370).jpg)

1890 map of Adelaide, showing the gradual development of its urban layout

[George Gawler](/wiki/George_Gawler "George Gawler") took over from Hindmarsh in late 1838 and, despite being under orders from the *Select Committee on South Australia* in Britain not to undertake any public works, promptly oversaw construction of a governor's house, the [Adelaide Gaol](/wiki/Adelaide_Gaol "Adelaide Gaol"), police barracks, a hospital, a customs house and a wharf at [Port Adelaide](/wiki/Port_Adelaide "Port Adelaide"). He was criticised for his response to the [Maria massacre](/wiki/Maria_massacre "Maria massacre"). Gawler was recalled and replaced by [George Edward Grey](/wiki/George_Edward_Grey "George Edward Grey") in 1841. Grey slashed public expenditure against heavy opposition, although its impact was negligible at this point: silver was discovered in [Glen Osmond](/wiki/Glen_Osmond "Glen Osmond") that year, agriculture was well underway, and other mines sprung up all over the state, aiding Adelaide's commercial development. The city exported meat, wool, wine, fruit and wheat by the time Grey left in 1845, contrasting with a low point in 1842 when one-third of Adelaide houses were abandoned.

Trade links with the rest of the Australian states were established after the [Murray River](/wiki/Murray_River "Murray River") was successfully navigated in 1853 by [Francis Cadell](/wiki/Francis_Cadell_(explorer) "Francis Cadell (explorer)"), an Adelaide resident. South Australia became a [self-governing colony](/wiki/Self-governing_colony "Self-governing colony") in 1856 with the ratification of a new constitution by the British parliament. [Secret ballots](/wiki/Secret_ballot "Secret ballot") were introduced, and a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") parliament was elected on 9 March 1857, by which time 109,917 people lived in the province.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

In 1860, the [Thorndon Park reservoir](/wiki/Thorndon_Park_Reserve "Thorndon Park Reserve") was opened, providing an alternative water source to the now turbid River Torrens. [Gas street lighting](/wiki/Gas_lighting "Gas lighting") was implemented in 1867, the [University of Adelaide](/wiki/University_of_Adelaide "University of Adelaide") was founded in 1874, the [South Australian Art Gallery](/wiki/South_Australian_Art_Gallery "South Australian Art Gallery") opened in 1881 and the [Happy Valley Reservoir](/wiki/Happy_Valley_Reservoir "Happy Valley Reservoir") opened in 1896. In the 1890s Australia was affected by a severe economic depression, ending a hectic era of land booms and tumultuous expansionism. Financial institutions in Melbourne and banks in Sydney closed. The national fertility rate fell and immigration was reduced to a trickle.[[51]](#cite_note-Guide-53)

The value of South Australia's exports nearly halved. Drought and poor harvests from 1884 compounded the problems, with some families leaving for Western Australia.[[51]](#cite_note-Guide-53) Adelaide was not as badly hit as the larger gold-rush cities of Sydney and Melbourne, and silver and lead discoveries at [Broken Hill](/wiki/Broken_Hill "Broken Hill") provided some relief. Only one year of deficit was recorded, but the price paid was retrenchments and lean public spending. Wine and copper were the only industries not to suffer a downturn.[[52]](#cite_note-54)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![Electric trams and motor cars at a crossroads in a densely built up area](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/North_Terrace_in_1938.jpg/250px-North_Terrace_in_1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_Terrace_in_1938.jpg)

The intersection of North Terrace and [King William Street](/wiki/King_William_Street,_Adelaide "King William Street, Adelaide") viewed from [Parliament House](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Adelaide "Parliament House, Adelaide"), 1938

[![Refer to caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Aerial_view_of_Adelaide%2C_1935_%28adjusted%29.jpg/250px-Aerial_view_of_Adelaide%2C_1935_%28adjusted%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_view_of_Adelaide,_1935_(adjusted).jpg)

An aerial view of Adelaide in 1935, when it was Australia's third largest city. Of note is that only the eastern half of the new Parliament House (to left of station) had been completed.

Adelaide was Australia's third largest city for most of the 20th century.[[53]](#cite_note-Marsden-55)[[54]](#cite_note-56)[[55]](#cite_note-57) [Electric street lighting](/wiki/Street_light "Street light") was introduced in 1900 and electric trams were transporting passengers in 1909. 28,000 men were sent to fight in World War I. Historian F. K. Crowley examined the reports of visitors in the early 20th century, noting that "many visitors to Adelaide admired the [foresighted planning](/wiki/Light%27s_Vision "Light's Vision") of its founders", as well as pondering the riches of the young city.[[56]](#cite_note-58)

Adelaide enjoyed a postwar boom, entering a time of relative prosperity. Its population grew, and it became the third most populous metropolitan area in the country, after Sydney and Melbourne. Its prosperity was short-lived, with the return of droughts and the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") of the 1930s. It later returned to fortune under strong government leadership. [Secondary industries](/wiki/Secondary_sector_of_industry "Secondary sector of industry") helped reduce the state's dependence on [primary industries](/wiki/Primary_sector_of_industry "Primary sector of industry"). World War II brought industrial stimulus and [diversification](/wiki/Economic_diversity "Economic diversity") to Adelaide under the [Playford](/wiki/Thomas_Playford_IV "Thomas Playford IV") Government, which advocated Adelaide as a safe place for manufacturing due to its less vulnerable location.[[57]](#cite_note-59) Shipbuilding was expanded at the nearby port of [Whyalla](/wiki/Whyalla,_South_Australia "Whyalla, South Australia").

The South Australian Government in this period built on former wartime manufacturing industries but neglected cultural facilities which meant South Australia's economy lagged behind.[[53]](#cite_note-Marsden-55) International manufacturers like [Holden](/wiki/Holden "Holden") and [Chrysler](/wiki/Fiat_Chrysler_Australia "Fiat Chrysler Australia")[[58]](#cite_note-60) made use of these factories around the Adelaide area in suburbs like [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth,_South_Australia "Elizabeth, South Australia"), completing its transformation from an [agricultural service centre](/wiki/Agricultural_economy "Agricultural economy") to a 20th-century motor city. The [Mannum–Adelaide pipeline](/wiki/Mannum%E2%80%93Adelaide_pipeline "Mannum–Adelaide pipeline") brought [River Murray](/wiki/River_Murray "River Murray") water to Adelaide in 1955 and [an airport](/wiki/Adelaide_Airport "Adelaide Airport") opened at [West Beach](/wiki/West_Beach,_South_Australia "West Beach, South Australia") in 1955. [Flinders University](/wiki/Flinders_University "Flinders University") and the [Flinders Medical Centre](/wiki/Flinders_Medical_Centre "Flinders Medical Centre") were established in the 1960s at Bedford Park, south of the city. Today, Flinders Medical Centre is one of the largest teaching hospitals in South Australia. In the post-war years around the early 1960s, Adelaide was surpassed by [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") as Australia's third largest city.[[53]](#cite_note-Marsden-55)

The [Dunstan Governments](/wiki/Don_Dunstan "Don Dunstan") of the 1970s saw something of an Adelaide "cultural revival",[[59]](#cite_note-61) establishing a wide array of social reforms. The city became noted for its [progressivism](/wiki/Progressivism "Progressivism") as South Australia became the first Australian state or territory to [decriminalise homosexuality](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_South_Australia "LGBT rights in South Australia") between [consenting](/wiki/Sexual_consent "Sexual consent") adults in 1975.[[60]](#cite_note-carbery-62) Adelaide became a centre for the arts, building upon the biennial "[Adelaide Festival of Arts](/wiki/Adelaide_Festival_of_Arts "Adelaide Festival of Arts")" that commenced in 1960. The [State Bank](/wiki/State_Bank_of_South_Australia "State Bank of South Australia") collapsed in 1991 during an economic recession. The effects lasted until 2004, when [Standard & Poor's](/wiki/Standard_%26_Poor%27s "Standard & Poor's") reinstated South Australia's AAA credit rating.[[61]](#cite_note-63) Adelaide's tallest building, completed in 2020, is called the Adelaidean and is located at 11 Frome Street.[[62]](#cite_note-64)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[![Adelaide City Skyline during 2022 Australia Day Celebrations Forefront: Torrens River, Elder Bank and Riverbank Precinct. From Right to Left: Stanford Hotel, Convention Centre, Myer Centre, The Switch, Realm Adelaide, Frome Central Tower One, GSA North Terrace, Schulz Building (Adelaide University).](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Adelaide_2022_Skyline.jpg/250px-Adelaide_2022_Skyline.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_2022_Skyline.jpg)

Adelaide's eastern skyline during 2022 [Australia Day](/wiki/Australia_Day "Australia Day") celebrations

In the early years of the 21st century, a significant increase in the state government's spending on Adelaide's infrastructure occurred. The [Rann government](/wiki/Mike_Rann "Mike Rann") invested A$535 million in a major upgrade of the Adelaide Oval to enable [Australian Football League](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League") to be played in the city centre[[63]](#cite_note-65) and more than A$2 [billion](/wiki/Billion "Billion") to build a new [Royal Adelaide Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Adelaide_Hospital "Royal Adelaide Hospital") on land adjacent to the Adelaide Railway Station.[[64]](#cite_note-66) The Glenelg tramline was extended through the city to Hindmarsh[[65]](#cite_note-67) down to East Terrace[[66]](#cite_note-68) and the suburban railway line extended south to Seaford.[[67]](#cite_note-69)

Following a period of stagnation in the 1990s and 2000s, Adelaide began several major developments and redevelopments. The Adelaide Convention Centre was redeveloped and expanded at a cost of A$350 million beginning in 2012.[[68]](#cite_note-70) Three historic buildings were adapted for modern use: the [Torrens Building](/wiki/Torrens_Building "Torrens Building") in Victoria Square as the Adelaide campus for Carnegie Mellon University, University College London, and Torrens University;[[69]](#cite_note-71) the Stock Exchange building as the Science Exchange of the Royal Institution Australia; and the Glenside Psychiatric Hospital as the Adelaide Studios of the [SA Film Corporation](/wiki/South_Australian_Film_Corporation "South Australian Film Corporation"). The government invested more than A$2 billion to build [a desalination plant](/wiki/Adelaide_Desalination_Plant "Adelaide Desalination Plant"), powered by renewable energy, as an "insurance policy" against droughts affecting Adelaide's [water supply](/wiki/Water_supply "Water supply").[[70]](#cite_note-72) The [Adelaide Festival](/wiki/Adelaide_Festival "Adelaide Festival"), [Fringe](/wiki/Adelaide_Fringe_Festival "Adelaide Fringe Festival"), and [Womadelaide](/wiki/Womadelaide "Womadelaide") became annual events.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Adelaide%2C_Australia_ESA384290.jpg/250px-Adelaide%2C_Australia_ESA384290.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adelaide,_Australia_ESA384290.jpg)

Adelaide's metropolitan area as seen by the [ESA](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency")'s [Sentinel-2](/wiki/Sentinel-2 "Sentinel-2")

Adelaide is north of the Fleurieu Peninsula, on the Adelaide Plains between the Gulf St Vincent and the relatively low-lying Mount Lofty Ranges (Mount Lofty, the highest point, is 710 metres above sea level). The city stretches 20 km (12 mi) from the coast to the foothills, and 90 km (56 mi) from [Gawler](/wiki/Gawler,_South_Australia "Gawler, South Australia") at its northern extent to [Sellicks Beach](/wiki/Sellicks_Beach,_South_Australia "Sellicks Beach, South Australia") in the south. According to Regional Development Australia, an Australian government planning initiative, the "Adelaide Metropolitan Region" has a total land area of 870 km2 (340 sq mi), while a more expansive definition by the Australian Bureau of Statistics defines a "Greater Adelaide" statistical area totalling 3,259.8 km2 (1,258.6 sq mi).[[8]](#cite_note-ABS-GA-8) The city sits at an average elevation of 50 metres (160 ft) above sea level. [Mount Lofty](/wiki/Mount_Lofty "Mount Lofty"), east of the Adelaide metropolitan region in the Adelaide Hills at an elevation of 727 metres (2,385 ft), is the tallest point of the city and in the state south of [Burra](/wiki/Burra,_South_Australia "Burra, South Australia"). The city borders the [Temperate Grassland of South Australia](/wiki/Temperate_Grassland_of_South_Australia "Temperate Grassland of South Australia") in the east, an endangered vegetation community.[[72]](#cite_note-74)

Much of Adelaide was bushland before British settlement, with some variation – sandhills, swamps and marshlands were prevalent around the coast. The loss of the sandhills to urban development had a particularly destructive effect on the coastline due to erosion.[[73]](#cite_note-75) Where practical, the government has implemented programs to rebuild and vegetate sandhills at several of Adelaide's beachside suburbs. [Tennyson Dunes](/wiki/Tennyson_Dunes_Conservation_Reserve "Tennyson Dunes Conservation Reserve") is the largest contiguous, tertiary dune system contained entirely within Metropolitan Adelaide, providing refuge for a variety of remnant species formerly found along the entire coastline.[[74]](#cite_note-76) Much of the original vegetation has been cleared with what is left to be found in reserves such as the [Cleland National Park](/wiki/Cleland_National_Park "Cleland National Park") and [Belair National Park](/wiki/Belair_National_Park "Belair National Park"). A number of creeks and rivers flow through the Adelaide region. The largest are the Torrens and [Onkaparinga](/wiki/Onkaparinga_River_National_Park "Onkaparinga River National Park") catchments. Adelaide relies on its many reservoirs for water supply with the [Happy Valley Reservoir](/wiki/Happy_Valley_Reservoir "Happy Valley Reservoir") supplying around 40% and the much larger [Mount Bold Reservoir](/wiki/Mount_Bold_Reservoir "Mount Bold Reservoir") 10% of Adelaide's domestic requirements respectively.

### Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geology")]

Adelaide and its surrounding area is one of the most seismically active regions in Australia. On 1 March 1954 at 3:40 am Adelaide experienced its largest recorded earthquake to date, with the epicentre 12 km from the city centre at [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington,_South_Australia "Darlington, South Australia"), and a reported magnitude of 5.6.[[75]](#cite_note-77)[[76]](#cite_note-78) There have been smaller earthquakes in 2010,[[77]](#cite_note-79) 2011,[[78]](#cite_note-80) 2014,[[79]](#cite_note-81) 2017,[[80]](#cite_note-82) 2018[[81]](#cite_note-83) and 2022.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

The uplands of the [Adelaide Hills](/wiki/Adelaide_Hills "Adelaide Hills"), part of the southern [Mount Lofty Ranges](/wiki/Mount_Lofty_Ranges "Mount Lofty Ranges") to the east of Adelaide, are defined on their western side by a number of arcuate faults (the Para, Eden, Clarendon and Willunga Faults), and consist of rocks such as [siltstone](/wiki/Siltstone "Siltstone"), [dolomite](/wiki/Dolomite_(rock) "Dolomite (rock)") and [quartzite](/wiki/Quartzite "Quartzite"), dating from the [Neoproterozoic](/wiki/Neoproterozoic "Neoproterozoic") to the middle [Cambrian](/wiki/Cambrian "Cambrian"), laid down in the Adelaide Rift Complex, the oldest part of the [Adelaide Superbasin](/wiki/Adelaide_Superbasin "Adelaide Superbasin").[[83]](#cite_note-85)

Most of the Adelaide metropolitan area lies in the downthrown St Vincent Basin and its embayments, including the [Adelaide Plains](/wiki/Adelaide_Plains "Adelaide Plains") Sub-basin, and the Golden Grove, Noarlunga and Willunga Embayments. These basins contain deposits of [Tertiary](/wiki/Tertiary_(period) "Tertiary (period)") marine and non-marine sands and limestones, which form important aquifers.[[84]](#cite_note-86) These deposits are overlain by [Quaternary](/wiki/Quaternary "Quaternary") [alluvial fans](/wiki/Alluvial_fan "Alluvial fan") and [piedmont](/wiki/Foothills "Foothills") slope deposits, derived from erosion of the uplands, consisting of sands, clays and gravels,[[85]](#cite_note-87) interfingering to the west with [transgressive](/wiki/Marine_transgression "Marine transgression") [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") to [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene") marine sands and coastal sediments of the shoreline of Gulf St Vincent.[[86]](#cite_note-88)

### Urban layout

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Urban layout")]

Further information: [William Light](/wiki/William_Light "William Light")

Adelaide is a planned city, designed by the first Surveyor-General of South Australia, Colonel [William Light](/wiki/William_Light "William Light"). His plan, sometimes referred to as "Light's Vision" (also the name of a statue of him on [Montefiore Hill](/wiki/Montefiore_Hill "Montefiore Hill")), arranged Adelaide in a [grid](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan"), with [five squares](/wiki/Category:Squares_in_Adelaide "Category:Squares in Adelaide") in the [Adelaide city centre](/wiki/Adelaide_city_centre "Adelaide city centre") and a ring of parks, known as the [Adelaide Parklands](/wiki/Adelaide_Parklands "Adelaide Parklands"), surrounding it. Light's selection of the location for the city was initially unpopular with the early settlers, as well as South Australia's first governor, John Hindmarsh, due to its distance from the harbour at Port Adelaide, and the lack of fresh water there.[[87]](#cite_note-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Adelaide_South_Australia_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Adelaide_South_Australia_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_South_Australia_-_panoramio.jpg)

The [city centre](/wiki/Adelaide_city_centre "Adelaide city centre") was built on a [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan"), known as "Light's Vision".

Light successfully persisted with his choice of location against this initial opposition. Recent evidence suggests that Light worked closely with George Kingston as well as a team of men to set out Adelaide, using various templates for city plans going back to [Ancient Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece"), including [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") designs and the similar layouts of the American cities [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and [Savannah](/wiki/Savannah,_Georgia "Savannah, Georgia")–which, like Adelaide, follow the same layout of a central city square, four complementing city squares surrounding it and a parklands area that surrounds the city centre.[[88]](#cite_note-90)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Transformers_-_Victoria_Square_Adelaide_SA_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Transformers_-_Victoria_Square_Adelaide_SA_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Transformers_-_Victoria_Square_Adelaide_SA_-_panoramio.jpg)

Aerial view of [Victoria Square](/wiki/Victoria_Square,_Adelaide "Victoria Square, Adelaide"), one of the five main squares in the city centre and considered the heart of Adelaide's [grid layout](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan")

The benefits of Light's design are numerous: Adelaide has had wide multi-lane roads from its beginning, an easily navigable [cardinal direction](/wiki/Cardinal_direction "Cardinal direction") grid layout and an expansive green ring around the city centre. There are two sets of [ring roads](/wiki/Ring_road "Ring road") in Adelaide that have resulted from the original design. The [inner ring route](/wiki/City_Ring_Route,_Adelaide "City Ring Route, Adelaide") ([A21](/wiki/A21_road_(Australia) "A21 road (Australia)")) borders the parklands, and the outer route (A3/[A13](/wiki/South_Road,_Adelaide "South Road, Adelaide")/[A16](/wiki/A16_highway_(Australia) "A16 highway (Australia)")/[A17](/wiki/A17_highway_(Australia) "A17 highway (Australia)")) completely bypasses the inner city via (in clockwise order) [Grand Junction Road](/wiki/Grand_Junction_Road "Grand Junction Road"), Hampstead Road, Ascot Avenue, [Portrush Road](/wiki/Portrush_Road "Portrush Road"), Cross Road and [South Road](/wiki/South_Road,_Adelaide "South Road, Adelaide").[[89]](#cite_note-91)

Suburban expansion has to some extent outgrown Light's original plan. Numerous former outlying villages and "country towns", as well as the satellite city of [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth,_South_Australia "Elizabeth, South Australia"), have been enveloped by its [suburban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl"). Expanding developments in the Adelaide Hills region led to the construction of the [South Eastern Freeway](/wiki/South_Eastern_Freeway "South Eastern Freeway") to cope with growth, which has subsequently led to new developments and further improvements to that transport corridor. Similarly, the booming development in Adelaide's [South](/wiki/City_of_Onkaparinga "City of Onkaparinga") led to the construction of the [Southern Expressway](/wiki/Southern_Expressway_(Australia) "Southern Expressway (Australia)").

New roads are not the only transport infrastructure developed to cope with the urban growth. The [O-Bahn Busway](/wiki/O-Bahn_Busway "O-Bahn Busway") and Adelaide Metro are examples of a unique solution to [Tea Tree Gully's](/wiki/Tea_Tree_Gully,_South_Australia "Tea Tree Gully, South Australia") transport woes in the 1980s.[[90]](#cite_note-92) The development of the nearby suburb of [Golden Grove](/wiki/Golden_Grove,_South_Australia "Golden Grove, South Australia") in the late 1980s followed a planned approach to urban growth.

In the 1960s, a [Metropolitan Adelaide Transport Study](/wiki/Metropolitan_Adelaide_Transport_Study "Metropolitan Adelaide Transport Study") Plan was proposed to cater for the future growth of the city. The plan involved the construction of freeways, [expressways](/wiki/Controlled-access_highway "Controlled-access highway") and the upgrade of certain aspects of the public transport system. The then premier [Steele Hall](/wiki/Steele_Hall_(Australian_politician) "Steele Hall (Australian politician)") approved many parts of the plan and the government went as far as purchasing land for the project. The later [Labor](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party") government elected under [Don Dunstan](/wiki/Don_Dunstan "Don Dunstan") shelved the plan, but allowed the purchased land to remain vacant, should the future need for freeways arise. In 1980, the [Liberal party](/wiki/Liberal_party_of_Australia "Liberal party of Australia") won government and premier [David Tonkin](/wiki/David_Tonkin "David Tonkin") committed his government to selling off the land acquired for the MATS plan, ensuring that even when needs changed, the construction of most MATS-proposed freeways would be impractical. Some parts of this land have been used for transport, (e.g. the O-Bahn Busway and Southern Expressway), while most has been progressively subdivided for residential use.

In 2008, the [SA Government](/wiki/Government_of_South_Australia "Government of South Australia") announced plans for a network of [transport-oriented developments](/wiki/Transport-oriented_development "Transport-oriented development") across the Adelaide metropolitan area and purchased a [10 hectare industrial site](/wiki/Clipsal_site_development "Clipsal site development") at [Bowden](/wiki/Bowden,_South_Australia "Bowden, South Australia") for $52.5 million as the first of these developments.[[91]](#cite_note-93)[[92]](#cite_note-94)

#### Housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Housing")]

Main article: [Australian residential architectural styles](/wiki/Australian_residential_architectural_styles "Australian residential architectural styles")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Heritage_terraces_on_Adelaide%27s_North_Terrace.jpg/250px-Heritage_terraces_on_Adelaide%27s_North_Terrace.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heritage_terraces_on_Adelaide%27s_North_Terrace.jpg)

Terraced housing on [North Terrace](/wiki/North_Terrace,_Adelaide "North Terrace, Adelaide")

Historically, Adelaide's suburban residential areas have been characterised by single-storey detached houses built on 1,000-square-metre (1⁄4-acre) blocks. A relative lack of suitable, locally available timber for construction purposes led to the early development of a brick-making industry, as well as the use of stone, for houses and other buildings. By 1891, 68% of houses were built of stone, 15% of timber, and 10% of brick, with brick also being widely used in stone houses for quoins, door and window surrounds, and chimneys and fireplaces.[[93]](#cite_note-95)

There is a wide variety in the styles of these houses. Until the 1960s, most of the more substantial houses were built of red brick, though many front walls were of ornamental stone. Then cream bricks became fashionable, and in the 1970s, deep red and brown bricks became popular.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Until the 1970s, roofs tended to be clad with (painted) [corrugated iron](/wiki/Corrugated_iron "Corrugated iron") or cement or clay tiles, usually red "terracotta". Since then, [Colorbond](/wiki/Colorbond "Colorbond") corrugated steel has dominated. Most roofs are pitched. Flat roofs are not common.[[94]](#cite_note-Cadden-96)

Up to the 1970s, most houses were of "double brick" construction on concrete footings, with timber floors laid on joists supported by "dwarf walls". Later houses have mainly been of "[brick veneer](/wiki/Brick_veneer "Brick veneer")" construction – structural [timber](/wiki/Framing_(construction) "Framing (construction)") or, more recently, [lightweight steel](/wiki/Steel_frame#Cold-formed_steel_frames "Steel frame") frame on a [concrete slab](/wiki/Concrete_slab "Concrete slab") [foundation](/wiki/Foundation_(engineering) "Foundation (engineering)"), lined with [Gyprock](/wiki/Gyprock "Gyprock"), and with an outer skin of brickwork,[[94]](#cite_note-Cadden-96) to cope with Adelaide's [reactive soils](/wiki/Shrink%E2%80%93swell_capacity "Shrink–swell capacity"), particularly Keswick Clay, black earth and some red-brown earth soils.[[95]](#cite_note-97) The use of precast concrete panels for floor and wall construction has also increased.[[94]](#cite_note-Cadden-96) In addition to this, a significant factor in Adelaide's suburban history is the role of the [South Australian Housing Trust](/wiki/South_Australian_Housing_Trust "South Australian Housing Trust").[*[why?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate")]

Main article: [Climate of Adelaide](/wiki/Climate_of_Adelaide "Climate of Adelaide")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Lightning_in_Adelaide%2C_South_Australia%2C_2014.jpg/250px-Lightning_in_Adelaide%2C_South_Australia%2C_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lightning_in_Adelaide,_South_Australia,_2014.jpg)

A spring storm over Adelaide

Adelaide has a hot-summer [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") (*Csa*) under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification").[[96]](#cite_note-98) The city has hot, dry summers and cool winters with moderate rainfall. Most rain falls in the winter months, leading to the suggestion that the climate be classified as a "cold monsoon".[[97]](#cite_note-99) Rainfall is unreliable, light and infrequent throughout summer, although heavy falls can occur. The winter has fairly reliable rainfall with June being the wettest month of the year, averaging around 80 mm. [Frosts](/wiki/Frost "Frost") are occasional, with the most notable occurrences in 1908 and 1982. Hail may occur in winter.

Adelaide is a windy city with significant [wind chill](/wiki/Wind_chill "Wind chill") in winter, which makes the temperature [seem colder than it actually is](/wiki/Apparent_temperature "Apparent temperature"). Snowfall in the metropolitan area is extremely rare, although light and sporadic falls in the nearby hills and at [Mount Lofty](/wiki/Mount_Lofty "Mount Lofty") occur during winter. Dewpoints in the summer typically range from 8 to 10 °C (46 to 50 °F). There are usually several days in summer where the temperature reaches 40.0 °C (104.0 °F) or above.

While conditions vary from year-to-year, a warming trend has been [increasing in recent years](/wiki/Climate_of_Adelaide#Climate_data_and_extremes "Climate of Adelaide"),[[98]](#cite_note-100)[[99]](#cite_note-101) and with drought conditions experienced in SA in 2024−25, Adelaide has had to rely on [desalination](/wiki/Adelaide_Desalination_Plant "Adelaide Desalination Plant") to augment its water supply.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

Temperature extremes range from −2.6 °C (31.4 °F), 8 June 1982 to 47.7 °C (117.9 °F), 24 January 2019. The city features 90.6 clear days annually.

The average sea temperature ranges from 13.7 °C (56.7 °F) in August to 21.2 °C (70.2 °F) in February.[[101]](#cite_note-103)

### Liveability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Liveability")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Rymill_Park_in_autumn.jpg/250px-Rymill_Park_in_autumn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rymill_Park_in_autumn.jpg)

[Rymill Park](/wiki/Rymill_Park,_Adelaide "Rymill Park, Adelaide") in autumn

Adelaide was consistently ranked in the world's 10 [most liveable cities](/wiki/Global_Liveability_Ranking "Global Liveability Ranking") through the 2010s by [The Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/The_Economist_Intelligence_Unit "The Economist Intelligence Unit").[[105]](#cite_note-108)[[106]](#cite_note-109)[[107]](#cite_note-110)[[108]](#cite_note-111)
In June 2021, *The Economist* ranked Adelaide the third most liveable city in the world, behind [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland") and [Osaka](/wiki/Osaka "Osaka").[[109]](#cite_note-112) In June 2023, Adelaide was ranked the twelfth most liveable city in the world by the Economist Intelligence Unit.[[110]](#cite_note-113)

In December 2021, Adelaide was named the world's second National Park City, after the state government had lobbied for this title.[[111]](#cite_note-114)[[112]](#cite_note-115)

It was ranked the [most liveable city](/wiki/Most_liveable_city "Most liveable city") in Australia by the [Property Council of Australia](/wiki/Property_Council_of_Australia "Property Council of Australia"), based on surveys of residents' views of their own city, between 2010 and 2013,[[113]](#cite_note-116)[[114]](#cite_note-117)[[115]](#cite_note-118) dropping to second place in 2014.[[116]](#cite_note-119)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Governance")]

Main article: [Government of South Australia](/wiki/Government_of_South_Australia "Government of South Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Parliament_House%2C_South_Australia.jpg/250px-Parliament_House%2C_South_Australia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parliament_House,_South_Australia.jpg)

[Parliament House, Adelaide](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Adelaide "Parliament House, Adelaide")

Adelaide, as the capital of South Australia, is the seat of the [Government of South Australia](/wiki/Government_of_South_Australia "Government of South Australia"). The [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameral "Bicameral") [Parliament of South Australia](/wiki/Parliament_of_South_Australia "Parliament of South Australia") consists of the [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") known as the [House of Assembly](/wiki/South_Australian_House_of_Assembly "South Australian House of Assembly") and the [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house") known as the [Legislative Council](/wiki/South_Australian_Legislative_Council "South Australian Legislative Council"). [General elections](/wiki/South_Australian_state_elections "South Australian state elections") are held every four years, the last being the [2022 South Australian state election](/wiki/2022_South_Australian_state_election "2022 South Australian state election").

As Adelaide is South Australia's capital and most populous city, the [State Government](/wiki/Government_of_South_Australia "Government of South Australia") co-operates extensively with the [City of Adelaide](/wiki/City_of_Adelaide "City of Adelaide"). In 2006, the Ministry for the City of Adelaide was created to facilitate the State Government's collaboration with the [Adelaide City Council](/wiki/Adelaide_City_Council "Adelaide City Council") and the Lord Mayor to improve Adelaide's image. The State Parliament's Capital City Committee is also involved in the governance of the City of Adelaide, being primarily concerned with the planning of Adelaide's urban development and growth.[[117]](#cite_note-120)

Reflecting South Australia's status as Australia's most centralised state, Adelaide elects a substantial majority of the South Australian House of Assembly. Of the 47 seats in the chamber, 34 seats (three-quarters of the legislature) are based in Adelaide, and two rural seats include Adelaide suburbs.

### Local governments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Local governments")]

Further information: [Local government areas of South Australia](/wiki/Local_government_areas_of_South_Australia "Local government areas of South Australia")

The Adelaide metropolitan area is divided between nineteen [local government areas](/wiki/Local_Government_Areas_of_South_Australia "Local Government Areas of South Australia"). At its centre, the [City of Adelaide](/wiki/City_of_Adelaide "City of Adelaide") administers the [Adelaide city centre](/wiki/Adelaide_city_centre "Adelaide city centre"), [North Adelaide](/wiki/North_Adelaide "North Adelaide"), and the surrounding [Adelaide Parklands](/wiki/Adelaide_Parklands "Adelaide Parklands"). It is the oldest municipal authority in Australia and was established in 1840, when Adelaide and Australia's first mayor, [James Hurtle Fisher](/wiki/James_Hurtle_Fisher "James Hurtle Fisher"), was elected. From 1919 onwards, the city has had a [Lord Mayor](/wiki/List_of_Mayors_and_Lord_Mayors_of_Adelaide "List of Mayors and Lord Mayors of Adelaide"), the current being Lord Mayor *The Right Honourable* [Jane Lomax-Smith](/wiki/Jane_Lomax-Smith "Jane Lomax-Smith").

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Demography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Adelaide_density.jpg/250px-Adelaide_density.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_density.jpg)

Adelaide's population density by mesh blocks (MB), 2016 census

Adelaide's inhabitants are known as Adelaideans.[[118]](#cite_note-Salt-121)[[119]](#cite_note-Adelaidean-122)

Compared with Australia's other state capitals, Adelaide is growing at a rate similar to Sydney and Hobart (see [List of cities in Australia by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Australia_by_population "List of cities in Australia by population")). In 2024, it had a metropolitan population (including suburbs) of 1,469,163,[[120]](#cite_note-GreaterAdelaide-123) making it Australia's fifth-largest city. 77%[[121]](#cite_note-124) of the population of South Australia are residents of the Adelaide metropolitan area, making South Australia one of the most centralised states.

Major areas of population growth in recent years have been in outer suburbs such as [Mawson Lakes](/wiki/Mawson_Lakes,_South_Australia "Mawson Lakes, South Australia") and Golden Grove. Adelaide's inhabitants occupy 366,912 houses, 57,695 semi-detached, row terrace or town houses and 49,413 flats, units or apartments.[[122]](#cite_note-ABS2011-125)

About one sixth (17.1%) of the population had university qualifications. The number of Adelaideans with vocational qualifications (such as tradespersons) fell from 62.1% of the labour force in the 1991 census to 52.4% in the 2001 census.

Adelaide is aging more rapidly than other Australian capital cities. More than a quarter (27.5%) of Adelaide's population is aged 55 years or older, in comparison to the national average of 25.6%. Adelaide has the lowest number of children (under-15-year-olds), who comprised 17.7% of the population, compared to the national average of 19.3%.[[122]](#cite_note-ABS2011-125)

### Ancestry and immigration

Overseas-born Adelaideans composed 31.3% of the total population at the 2021 census. The five largest groups of overseas-born were from England (5.7%), India (3.1%), [Mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") (1.8%), Vietnam (1.2%) and Italy (1.1%).[[125]](#cite_note-auto1-131)

Suburbs including [Newton](/wiki/Newton,_South_Australia "Newton, South Australia"), [Payneham](/wiki/Payneham,_South_Australia "Payneham, South Australia") and [Campbelltown](/wiki/Campbelltown,_South_Australia "Campbelltown, South Australia") in the east and [Torrensville](/wiki/Torrensville,_South_Australia "Torrensville, South Australia"), [West Lakes](/wiki/West_Lakes,_South_Australia "West Lakes, South Australia") and [Fulham](/wiki/Fulham,_South_Australia "Fulham, South Australia") to the west, have large [Greek](/wiki/Greek_Australian "Greek Australian") and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Australian "Italian Australian") communities. The Italian consulate is located in the western suburb of [Hindmarsh](/wiki/Hindmarsh,_South_Australia "Hindmarsh, South Australia").[[126]](#cite_note-132) Large [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_Australian "Vietnamese Australian") populations are settled in the north-western suburbs of [Woodville](/wiki/Woodville,_South_Australia "Woodville, South Australia"), [Kilkenny](/wiki/Kilkenny,_South_Australia "Kilkenny, South Australia"), [Pennington](/wiki/Pennington,_South_Australia "Pennington, South Australia"), [Mansfield Park](/wiki/Mansfield_Park,_South_Australia "Mansfield Park, South Australia") and [Athol Park](/wiki/Athol_Park,_South_Australia "Athol Park, South Australia") and also [Parafield Gardens](/wiki/Parafield_Gardens,_South_Australia "Parafield Gardens, South Australia") and [Pooraka](/wiki/Pooraka,_South_Australia "Pooraka, South Australia") in Adelaide's north. Migrants from [India](/wiki/Indian_Australian "Indian Australian") and [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lankan_Australian "Sri Lankan Australian") have settled into inner suburban areas of Adelaide including the inner northern suburbs of [Blair Athol](/wiki/Blair_Athol,_South_Australia "Blair Athol, South Australia"), [Kilburn](/wiki/Kilburn,_South_Australia "Kilburn, South Australia") and [Enfield](/wiki/Enfield,_South_Australia "Enfield, South Australia") and the inner southern suburbs of [Plympton](/wiki/Plympton,_South_Australia "Plympton, South Australia"), [Park Holme](/wiki/Park_Holme,_South_Australia "Park Holme, South Australia") and [Kurralta Park](/wiki/Kurralta_Park,_South_Australia "Kurralta Park, South Australia").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Suburbs such as [Para Hills](/wiki/Para_Hills,_South_Australia "Para Hills, South Australia"), [Salisbury](/wiki/Salisbury,_South_Australia "Salisbury, South Australia"), [Ingle Farm](/wiki/Ingle_Farm,_South_Australia "Ingle Farm, South Australia") and [Blair Athol](/wiki/Blair_Athol,_South_Australia "Blair Athol, South Australia") in the north and [Findon](/wiki/Findon,_South_Australia "Findon, South Australia"), [West Croydon](/wiki/West_Croydon,_South_Australia "West Croydon, South Australia") and [Seaton](/wiki/Seaton,_South_Australia "Seaton, South Australia") and other Western suburbs have sizeable [Afghan](/wiki/Afghan_Australian "Afghan Australian") communities. [Chinese migrants](/wiki/Chinese_Australian "Chinese Australian") favour settling in the eastern and north eastern suburbs including [Kensington Gardens](/wiki/Kensington_Gardens,_South_Australia "Kensington Gardens, South Australia"), [Greenacres](/wiki/Greenacres,_South_Australia "Greenacres, South Australia"), [Modbury](/wiki/Modbury,_South_Australia "Modbury, South Australia") and [Golden Grove](/wiki/Golden_Grove,_South_Australia "Golden Grove, South Australia"). [Mawson Lakes](/wiki/Mawson_Lakes,_South_Australia "Mawson Lakes, South Australia") has a large international student population, due to its proximity to the [University of South Australia](/wiki/University_of_South_Australia "University of South Australia") campus.[[127]](#cite_note-133)

At the 2021 census, 1.7% of Adelaide's population identified as being [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_Australians "Indigenous Australians") — [Aboriginal Australians](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") and [Torres Strait Islanders](/wiki/Torres_Strait_Islanders "Torres Strait Islanders").[[N 2]](#cite_note-134)[[125]](#cite_note-auto1-131)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Language")]

At the 2016 census, 75.4% of the population spoke [English](/wiki/Australian_English "Australian English") at home. The other languages most commonly spoken at home were Italian (2.1%), [Standard Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Mandarin "Standard Mandarin") (2.1%), Greek (1.7%) Vietnamese (1.4%), and [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") (0.7%).[[128]](#cite_note-quickstats.censusdata.abs.gov.au-135) The [Kaurna language](/wiki/Kaurna_language "Kaurna language"), spoken by the area's original inhabitants, had no living speakers in the middle of the 20th century, but since the 1990s there has been a sustained revival effort from academics and Kaurna elders.[[129]](#cite_note-136)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/St._Nicholas_Church%2C_Wayville.jpg/250px-St._Nicholas_Church%2C_Wayville.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Nicholas_Church,_Wayville.jpg)

St Nicholas Church, a Russian Orthodox church in [Wayville](/wiki/Wayville,_South_Australia "Wayville, South Australia"). Adelaide's 19th century moniker was *The City of Churches*.[[130]](#cite_note-137)

Adelaide was founded on a vision of religious tolerance that attracted a wide variety of religious practitioners. This led to it being known as *The City of Churches*.[[131]](#cite_note-138)[[132]](#cite_note-139)[[133]](#cite_note-140) But approximately 28% of the population expressed no religious affiliation in the 2011 Census, compared with the national average of 22.3%, making Adelaide one of Australia's least religious cities.[[134]](#cite_note-141) According to 2021 census, 39.8% of the population of Adelaide identify as Christian, with the largest denominations being [Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church_in_Australia "Roman Catholic Church in Australia") (16.4%), [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Australia "Anglican Church of Australia") (7.0%), [Uniting Church](/wiki/Uniting_Church_in_Australia "Uniting Church in Australia") (3.9%) and [Greek Orthodox](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church "Greek Orthodox Church") (2.4%). Non-Christian faith communities, representing 9.5% from Adelaide's population, include [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (2.8%), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") (2.7%) and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (2.3%).[[135]](#cite_note-142)

The Jewish community of the city dates back to 1840. Eight years later, 58 Jews lived in the city.[[136]](#cite_note-JVL-143) A synagogue was built in 1871, when 435 Jews lived in the city. Many took part in the city councils, such as [Judah Moss Solomon](/wiki/Judah_Moss_Solomon "Judah Moss Solomon") (1852–66). Three Jews have been elected to the position of [city mayor](/wiki/List_of_mayors_and_lord_mayors_of_Adelaide "List of mayors and lord mayors of Adelaide").[[137]](#cite_note-144) In 1968, the Jewish population of Adelaide numbered about 1,200;[[138]](#cite_note-145) in 2001, according to the Australian census, 979 persons declared themselves to be Jewish by religion.[[136]](#cite_note-JVL-143) In 2011, over 1,000 Jews were living in the city, served by an [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") synagogue, [Adelaide Hebrew Congregation](/wiki/Adelaide_Hebrew_Congregation "Adelaide Hebrew Congregation") and a [Reform](/wiki/Reform_Judaism "Reform Judaism") synagogue, **Beit Shalom**, in addition to a virtual Jewish museum. [Massada College](/wiki/Massada_College,_Adelaide "Massada College, Adelaide"), a Jewish day school opened in the city in 1976 and closed in 2011.[[139]](#cite_note-146)[[140]](#cite_note-147) The [Adelaide Holocaust Museum and Andrew Steiner Education Centre](/wiki/Adelaide_Holocaust_Museum_and_Andrew_Steiner_Education_Centre "Adelaide Holocaust Museum and Andrew Steiner Education Centre") opened in 2020.[[141]](#cite_note-148)

The "[Afghan](/wiki/Afghan_(Australia) "Afghan (Australia)")" community in Australia first became established in the 1860s when camels and their Pathan, Punjabi, Baluchi and Sindhi handlers began to be used to open up settlement in the continent's arid interior.[[142]](#cite_note-149) Until eventually superseded by the advent of the railways and motor vehicles, camels played an invaluable economic and social role in transporting heavy loads of goods to and from isolated settlements and mines. This is acknowledged by the name of [The Ghan](/wiki/The_Ghan "The Ghan"), the passenger train operating between Adelaide, Alice Springs, and Darwin. The [Central Adelaide Mosque](/wiki/Central_Adelaide_Mosque "Central Adelaide Mosque") is regarded as Australia's oldest permanent mosque; an earlier [mosque at Marree](/wiki/Marree_Mosque "Marree Mosque") in northern South Australia, dating from 1861 to 1862 and subsequently abandoned or demolished, has now been rebuilt.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/NRAHfront.jpg/250px-NRAHfront.jpg)](/wiki/File:NRAHfront.jpg)

The new [Royal Adelaide Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Adelaide_Hospital "Royal Adelaide Hospital") opened in 2017. Health care and social assistance is the largest [ABS](/wiki/Australian_Bureau_of_Statistics "Australian Bureau of Statistics")-defined employment sector in South Australia.[[143]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-150)

South Australia's largest employment sectors are health care and social assistance,[[143]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-150)[[144]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-151) surpassing manufacturing in SA as the largest employer since 2006–07.[[143]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-150)[[144]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-151) In 2009–10, manufacturing in SA had average annual employment of 83,700 persons compared with 103,300 for health care and social assistance.[[143]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-150) Health care and social assistance represented nearly 13% of the state average annual employment.[[145]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-152) The [Adelaide Hills wine region](/wiki/Adelaide_Hills_wine_region "Adelaide Hills wine region") is an iconic and viable economic region for both the state and country in terms of wine production and sale. The 2014 vintage is reported as consisting of 5,836 t (5,744 long tons; 6,433 short tons) red grapes crushed valued at A$8,196,142 and 12,037 t (11,847 long tons; 13,269 short tons) white grapes crushed valued at $14,777,631.[[146]](#cite_note-PGIBSA25-153)

The retail trade is the second largest employer in SA (2009–10), with over 91,900 jobs, and 12 per cent of the state workforce.[[145]](#cite_note-autogenerated3-152)

Manufacturing, defence technology, high-tech electronic systems and research, commodity export and corresponding service industries all play a role in the SA economy. Almost half of all cars produced in Australia were made in Adelaide at the [Holden Elizabeth Plant](/wiki/Holden_Elizabeth_Plant "Holden Elizabeth Plant") in [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth,_South_Australia "Elizabeth, South Australia").[[147]](#cite_note-154) The site ceased operating in November 2017.

The [collapse of the State Bank in 1992](/wiki/State_Bank_of_South_Australia "State Bank of South Australia") resulted in large levels of state public debt (as much as A$4 billion). The collapse meant that successive governments enacted lean budgets, cutting spending, which was a setback to the further economic development of the city and state. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]As of January 2026, the government has an AA+ credit rating.

The global media conglomerate [News Corporation](/wiki/News_Corporation "News Corporation") was founded in, and until 2004 incorporated in, Adelaide, and the city is still considered the Corporation's "spiritual" home by its founder, [Rupert Murdoch](/wiki/Rupert_Murdoch "Rupert Murdoch").[[148]](#cite_note-155) Australia's largest oil company, [Santos](/wiki/Santos_Limited "Santos Limited"), prominent South Australian brewery, [Coopers](/wiki/Coopers_Brewery "Coopers Brewery"), and national retailer [Harris Scarfe](/wiki/Harris_Scarfe "Harris Scarfe") also call Adelaide their home.

In 2018, at which time more than 80 organisations employed 800 people in the space sector in South Australia, Adelaide was chosen for the headquarters of a new [Australian Space Agency](/wiki/Australian_Space_Agency "Australian Space Agency").[[149]](#cite_note-156) The agency opened its in 2020. It is working to triple the size of the Australian space industry and create 20,000 new jobs by 2030.[[150]](#cite_note-157)

### Defence industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Defence industry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/US_Navy_040823-N-3019M-003_The_Australian_Collins-class_submarine%2C_HMAS_Rankin_%28SSK_78%29%2C_enters_Pearl_Harbor_for_a_port_visit_after_completing_exercises_in_the_Pacific_region.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_040823-N-3019M-003_The_Australian_Collins-class_submarine,_HMAS_Rankin_(SSK_78),_enters_Pearl_Harbor_for_a_port_visit_after_completing_exercises_in_the_Pacific_region.jpg)

The Adelaide-built [*Collins*-class](/wiki/Collins-class_submarine "Collins-class submarine") submarine [HMAS *Rankin*](/wiki/HMAS_Rankin "HMAS Rankin") entering [Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Pearl_Harbor "Pearl Harbor"), August 2004

Adelaide is home to a large proportion of Australia's defence industries, which contribute over A$1 billion to South Australia's Gross State Product.[[151]](#cite_note-158) The principal government military research institution, the [Defence Science and Technology Organisation](/wiki/Defence_Science_and_Technology_Organisation "Defence Science and Technology Organisation"), and other defence technology organisations such as [BAE Systems Australia](/wiki/BAE_Systems_Australia "BAE Systems Australia") and Lockheed Martin Australia, are north of Salisbury and west of Elizabeth in an area now called "Edinburgh Parks", adjacent to [RAAF Base Edinburgh](/wiki/RAAF_Base_Edinburgh "RAAF Base Edinburgh").

Others, such as Saab Systems and Raytheon, are in or near [Technology Park](/wiki/Technology_Park,_Adelaide "Technology Park, Adelaide"). [ASC Pty Ltd](/wiki/ASC_Pty_Ltd "ASC Pty Ltd"), is based in the industrial suburb of [Osborne](/wiki/Osborne,_South_Australia "Osborne, South Australia") and is also a part of [Technology Park](/wiki/Technology_Park,_Adelaide "Technology Park, Adelaide"). South Australia was charged with constructing Australia's [*Collins*-class submarines](/wiki/Collins-class_submarine "Collins-class submarine") and more recently the A$6 billion contract to construct the [Royal Australian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Navy "Royal Australian Navy")'s new [air-warfare destroyers](/wiki/Hobart-class_destroyer "Hobart-class destroyer").[[152]](#cite_note-159)

### Employment statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Employment statistics")]

As of November 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit), Greater Adelaide had an unemployment rate of 7.4% with a youth unemployment rate of 15%.[[153]](#cite_note-160)

The median weekly individual income for people aged 15 years and over was $447 per week in 2006, compared with $466 nationally. The median family income was $1,137 per week, compared with $1,171 nationally.[[154]](#cite_note-ABS-161) Adelaide's housing and living costs are substantially lower than that of other Australian cities, with housing being notably cheaper. The median Adelaide house price is half that of Sydney and two-thirds that of Melbourne. The three-month trend unemployment rate to March 2007 was 6.2%.[[155]](#cite_note-162) The Northern suburbs' unemployment rate is disproportionately higher than the other regions of Adelaide at 8.3%, while the East and South are lower than the Adelaide average at 4.9% and 5.0% respectively.[[156]](#cite_note-163)
