## Education and research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Education and research")]

Main article: [Education in South Australia](/wiki/South_Australia#Education "South Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Adelaide_%28AU%29%2C_Barr_Smith_Library_--_2019_--_0677.jpg/250px-Adelaide_%28AU%29%2C_Barr_Smith_Library_--_2019_--_0677.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_(AU),_Barr_Smith_Library_--_2019_--_0677.jpg)

[Barr Smith Library](/wiki/Barr_Smith_Library "Barr Smith Library"), part of the [University of Adelaide](/wiki/University_of_Adelaide "University of Adelaide")

Education forms an increasingly important part of the city's economy, with the [South Australian Government](/wiki/Government_of_South_Australia "Government of South Australia") and educational institutions attempting to position Adelaide as "Australia's education hub" and marketing it as a "Learning City".[[163]](#cite_note-eduhub-170) The number of international students studying in Adelaide has increased rapidly in recent years to 30,726 in 2015, of which 1,824 were secondary school students.[[164]](#cite_note-thecity-171)
Adelaide is the birthplace of three Nobel laureates, [more than any other Australian city](/wiki/List_of_Australian_Nobel_laureates#Australian_laureates_by_birthplace "List of Australian Nobel laureates"): physicist [William Lawrence Bragg](/wiki/William_Lawrence_Bragg "William Lawrence Bragg") and pathologists [Howard Florey](/wiki/Howard_Florey "Howard Florey") and [Robin Warren](/wiki/Robin_Warren "Robin Warren"), all of whom completed secondary and tertiary education at [St Peter's College](/wiki/St_Peter%27s_College,_Adelaide "St Peter's College, Adelaide") and the [University of Adelaide](/wiki/University_of_Adelaide "University of Adelaide").

Adelaide is also the hometown of mathematician [Terence Tao](/wiki/Terence_Tao "Terence Tao").[[165]](#cite_note-172)

### Primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Primary and secondary education")]

There are two systems of primary and secondary schools, a public system operated by the South Australian Government's [Department for Education](/wiki/Department_for_Education_(South_Australia) "Department for Education (South Australia)"), and a private system of independent and Catholic schools.[[166]](#cite_note-173) [South Australian schools](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_South_Australia "List of schools in South Australia") provide education under the [Australian Curriculum](/wiki/Australian_Curriculum "Australian Curriculum") for reception to Year 10 students. In Years 10 to 12, students study for the [South Australian Certificate of Education](/wiki/South_Australian_Certificate_of_Education "South Australian Certificate of Education") (SACE). They have the option of incorporating [vocational education and training](/wiki/Vocational_education "Vocational education") (VET) courses or a flexible learning option (FLO).[[167]](#cite_note-174) South Australia also has 24 schools that use [International Baccalaureate](/wiki/International_Baccalaureate "International Baccalaureate") programs as an alternative to the Australian Curriculum or SACE. These programs include the [IB Primary Years Programme](/wiki/IB_Primary_Years_Programme "IB Primary Years Programme"), the [IB Middle Years Programme](/wiki/IB_Middle_Years_Programme "IB Middle Years Programme"), and the [IB Diploma Programme](/wiki/IB_Diploma_Programme "IB Diploma Programme").[[168]](#cite_note-175)

For South Australian students who cannot attend a traditional school, including students who live in rural or remote areas, the state government runs the Open Access College (OAC), which provides virtual teaching. The OAC has a campus in [Marden](/wiki/Marden,_South_Australia "Marden, South Australia") which caters to students from reception to Year 12 and adults who haven't been able to complete their SACE.[[169]](#cite_note-176)[[170]](#cite_note-177) Guardians are also able to apply for their child to be educated from home as long as they provide an education program which meets the same requirements as the Australian Curriculum as well as opportunities for social interaction.[[171]](#cite_note-178)

### Tertiary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Tertiary education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Torrens_Building%2C_Wakefield_St%2C_Adelaide.JPG/250px-Torrens_Building%2C_Wakefield_St%2C_Adelaide.JPG)](/wiki/File:Torrens_Building,_Wakefield_St,_Adelaide.JPG)

Historic Torrens Building in [Victoria Square](/wiki/Victoria_Square,_Adelaide "Victoria Square, Adelaide") houses campuses of several international universities operating in South Australia

There are three public universities local to Adelaide, as well as one private university and three constituent colleges of foreign universities. [Flinders University of South Australia](/wiki/Flinders_University_of_South_Australia "Flinders University of South Australia"), the [University of Adelaide](/wiki/University_of_Adelaide "University of Adelaide"), the [University of South Australia](/wiki/University_of_South_Australia "University of South Australia") and [Torrens University Australia](/wiki/Torrens_University_Australia "Torrens University Australia")—part of the [Laureate International Universities](/wiki/Laureate_International_Universities "Laureate International Universities") are based in Adelaide. The University of Adelaide was ranked in the top 150 universities worldwide. Flinders ranked in the top 250 and Uni SA in the top 300. Torrens University Australia is part of an international network of over 70 higher education institutions in more than 30 countries worldwide.

The [University of Adelaide](/wiki/University_of_Adelaide "University of Adelaide"), with 25,000 students,[[172]](#cite_note-179) is Australia's third-oldest university and a member of the leading "[Group of Eight](/wiki/Group_of_Eight_(Australian_universities) "Group of Eight (Australian universities)")". It has five campuses throughout the state, including two in the city-centre, and a campus in Singapore. The [University of South Australia](/wiki/University_of_South_Australia "University of South Australia"), with 37,000 students,[[173]](#cite_note-180) has two North Terrace campuses, three other campuses in the metropolitan area and campuses in the regional cities of [Whyalla](/wiki/Whyalla "Whyalla") and [Mount Gambier](/wiki/Mount_Gambier "Mount Gambier"). The University of Adelaide and the University of South Australia have had multiple proposals to merge into a single university. A proposal in 2018 failed due to uncertainty as to the new name and leadership of the merged university.[[174]](#cite_note-181) In 2022, the universities announced a new merger proposal, with the name and leadership issues settled and support from the South Australian government.[[175]](#cite_note-182)

[Flinders University](/wiki/Flinders_University "Flinders University"), with 25,184 students,[[176]](#cite_note-183) is based in the southern suburb of [Bedford Park](/wiki/Bedford_Park,_South_Australia "Bedford Park, South Australia"), alongside the [Flinders Medical Centre](/wiki/Flinders_Medical_Centre "Flinders Medical Centre"), with additional campuses in neighbouring [Tonsley](/wiki/Tonsley "Tonsley") and in Victoria Square in the city centre.

In 2024, the [University of the Sunshine Coast](/wiki/University_of_the_Sunshine_Coast "University of the Sunshine Coast") opened a new campus in Adelaide where undergraduate and master's courses in [ICT](/wiki/Information_and_communications_technology "Information and communications technology") and business are offered.[[177]](#cite_note-p920-184)[[178]](#cite_note-d156-185)

The [Adelaide College of Divinity](/wiki/Adelaide_College_of_Divinity "Adelaide College of Divinity") is at [Brooklyn Park](/wiki/Brooklyn_Park,_South_Australia "Brooklyn Park, South Australia").

There are several South Australian [TAFE](/wiki/TAFE_South_Australia "TAFE South Australia") (Technical and Further Education) campuses in the metropolitan area that provide a range of vocational education and training. The [Adelaide College of the Arts](/wiki/Adelaide_College_of_the_Arts "Adelaide College of the Arts"), as a school of TAFE SA, provides nationally recognised training in visual and performing arts.

StudyAdelaide, a collaboration between the South Australian government and the tertiary education sector, maintains an on-line list of schools, universities, and higher education institutions in SA.[[179]](#cite_note-186)

* The east end of [North Terrace](/wiki/North_Terrace,_Adelaide "North Terrace, Adelaide"): [SA Pathology](/wiki/SA_Pathology "SA Pathology");[[181]](#cite_note-188) [Hanson Institute](/wiki/Hanson_Institute "Hanson Institute");[[182]](#cite_note-189) [National Wine Centre](/wiki/National_Wine_Centre_of_Australia "National Wine Centre of Australia").
* The west end of North Terrace: [South Australian Health and Medical Research Institute](/wiki/South_Australian_Health_and_Medical_Research_Institute "South Australian Health and Medical Research Institute") (SAHMRI), located next to the [Royal Adelaide Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Adelaide_Hospital "Royal Adelaide Hospital").
* The [Waite Research Precinct](/wiki/Waite_Research_Precinct "Waite Research Precinct"): [SARDI](/wiki/South_Australian_Research_and_Development_Institute "South Australian Research and Development Institute") Head Office and Plant Research Centre; [AWRI](/wiki/Australian_Wine_Research_Institute "Australian Wine Research Institute");[[183]](#cite_note-190) [ACPFG](/wiki/Australian_Centre_for_Plant_Functional_Genomics "Australian Centre for Plant Functional Genomics");[[184]](#cite_note-191) [CSIRO](/wiki/CSIRO "CSIRO") research laboratories.[[185]](#cite_note-CSIROWaite-192) SARDI also has establishments at [Glenside](/wiki/Glenside,_South_Australia "Glenside, South Australia")[[186]](#cite_note-193) and [West Beach](/wiki/West_Beach,_South_Australia "West Beach, South Australia").[[187]](#cite_note-194)

## Cultural life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Cultural life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Art_Gallery_of_South_Australia%2C_North_Terrace%2C_Adelaide.jpg/250px-Art_Gallery_of_South_Australia%2C_North_Terrace%2C_Adelaide.jpg)](/wiki/File:Art_Gallery_of_South_Australia,_North_Terrace,_Adelaide.jpg)

The [Art Gallery of South Australia](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_South_Australia "Art Gallery of South Australia") on North Terrace

See also: [South Australia § Cultural life](/wiki/South_Australia#Cultural_life "South Australia"), [Department of the Premier and Cabinet (South Australia) § Arts and culture](/wiki/Department_of_the_Premier_and_Cabinet_(South_Australia)#Arts_and_culture "Department of the Premier and Cabinet (South Australia)"), and [List of festivals in Australia § South Australia](/wiki/List_of_festivals_in_Australia#South_Australia "List of festivals in Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/ACC_-_NYE_low_res-69.jpg/250px-ACC_-_NYE_low_res-69.jpg)](/wiki/File:ACC_-_NYE_low_res-69.jpg)

New Year's Eve celebrations in [Elder Park](/wiki/Elder_Park "Elder Park") on the River Torrens (mid right), Adelaide Oval (further, left) and Adelaide Festival Centre (near right) are also in view.

While established as a [British province](/wiki/British_colonisation_of_South_Australia "British colonisation of South Australia"), and very much English in terms of its culture, Adelaide attracted immigrants from other parts of Europe early on, including German and other European non-conformists escaping religious persecution. The first [German Lutherans arrived in 1838](/wiki/German_Australians#Old_Lutherans "German Australians"),[[189]](#cite_note-196) bringing with them the [vine cuttings](/wiki/Vine_cuttings "Vine cuttings") that they used to found the acclaimed wineries of the [Barossa Valley](/wiki/Barossa_Valley "Barossa Valley").

The [Royal Adelaide Show](/wiki/Royal_Adelaide_Show "Royal Adelaide Show") is an annual [agricultural show](/wiki/Agricultural_show "Agricultural show") and [state fair](/wiki/State_fair "State fair"), established in 1839 and now a huge event held in the [Adelaide Showground](/wiki/Adelaide_Showground "Adelaide Showground") annually.

Adelaide's arts scene flourished in the 1960s and 1970s with the support of successive premiers from both major political parties. The renowned [Adelaide Festival of Arts](/wiki/Adelaide_Festival_of_Arts "Adelaide Festival of Arts") was established in 1960 under Thomas Playford, which in the same year spawned an unofficial uncurated series of performances and exhibits which grew into the [Adelaide Fringe](/wiki/Adelaide_Fringe "Adelaide Fringe"). Construction of the [Adelaide Festival Centre](/wiki/Adelaide_Festival_Centre "Adelaide Festival Centre") began under Steele Hall in 1970 and was completed under the subsequent government of [Don Dunstan](/wiki/Don_Dunstan "Don Dunstan"), who also established the [South Australian Film Corporation](/wiki/South_Australian_Film_Corporation "South Australian Film Corporation") in 1972 and the [State Opera of South Australia](/wiki/State_Opera_of_South_Australia "State Opera of South Australia") in 1976.

Over time, the Adelaide Festival expanded to include [Adelaide Writers' Week](/wiki/Adelaide_Writers%27_Week "Adelaide Writers' Week") and [WOMADelaide](/wiki/WOMADelaide "WOMADelaide"), and other separate festivals were established, such as the [Adelaide Cabaret Festival](/wiki/Adelaide_Cabaret_Festival "Adelaide Cabaret Festival") (2002), the [Adelaide Festival of Ideas](/wiki/Adelaide_Festival_of_Ideas "Adelaide Festival of Ideas") (1999), the [Adelaide Film Festival](/wiki/Adelaide_Film_Festival "Adelaide Film Festival") (2013), [FEAST](/wiki/Feast_Festival "Feast Festival") (1999, a [queer culture](/wiki/LGBT_culture "LGBT culture")), [Tasting Australia](/wiki/Tasting_Australia "Tasting Australia") (1997, a food and wine affair), and [Illuminate Adelaide](/wiki/Illuminate_Adelaide "Illuminate Adelaide") (2021). With the Festival, the Fringe, WOMADelaide, Writers' Week and the [Adelaide 500](/wiki/Adelaide_500 "Adelaide 500") street motor racing event (along with evening music concerts) all happening in early March, the period became known colloquially as "Mad March".

In 2014, [Ghil'ad Zuckermann](/wiki/Ghil%27ad_Zuckermann "Ghil'ad Zuckermann") founded the [Adelaide Language Festival](/wiki/Adelaide_Language_Festival "Adelaide Language Festival").[[190]](#cite_note-197)[[191]](#cite_note-198)

There are many international cultural fairs, most notably the German [Schützenfest](/wiki/Sch%C3%BCtzenfest_(Adelaide) "Schützenfest (Adelaide)") and Greek [Glendi](/wiki/Glendi "Glendi"). Adelaide holds an annual [Christmas pageant](/wiki/Adelaide_Christmas_Pageant "Adelaide Christmas Pageant"), the world's largest [Christmas parade](/wiki/Santa_Claus_parade "Santa Claus parade").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Palm_House%2C_Adelaide_Botanic_Gardens.jpg/250px-Palm_House%2C_Adelaide_Botanic_Gardens.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palm_House,_Adelaide_Botanic_Gardens.jpg)

Palm House at the [Adelaide Botanic Garden](/wiki/Adelaide_Botanic_Garden "Adelaide Botanic Garden")

### North Terrace institutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: North Terrace institutions")]

As the state capital, Adelaide has a great number of cultural institutions, many of them along the boulevard of [North Terrace](/wiki/North_Terrace,_Adelaide "North Terrace, Adelaide"). The [Art Gallery of South Australia](/wiki/Art_Gallery_of_South_Australia "Art Gallery of South Australia"), with about 35,000 works, holds Australia's second largest state-based collection. Adjacent are the [South Australian Museum](/wiki/South_Australian_Museum "South Australian Museum") and [State Library of South Australia](/wiki/State_Library_of_South_Australia "State Library of South Australia"). The [Adelaide Botanic Garden](/wiki/Adelaide_Botanic_Garden "Adelaide Botanic Garden"), [National Wine Centre](/wiki/National_Wine_Centre "National Wine Centre") and [Tandanya National Aboriginal Cultural Institute](/wiki/Tandanya_National_Aboriginal_Cultural_Institute "Tandanya National Aboriginal Cultural Institute") are nearby in the [East End](/wiki/East_End,_Adelaide "East End, Adelaide") of the city. In the back of the State Library lies the [Migration Museum](/wiki/Migration_Museum,_Adelaide "Migration Museum, Adelaide"), Australia's oldest museum of its kind.

Further west, the [Lion Arts Centre](/wiki/Lion_Arts_Centre "Lion Arts Centre") is home to [ACE Open](/wiki/ACE_Open "ACE Open"), which showcases contemporary art; [Dance Hub SA](/wiki/Dance_Hub_SA "Dance Hub SA"); and other studios and arts industry spaces. The [Mercury Cinema](/wiki/Mercury_Cinema "Mercury Cinema") and the [JamFactory](/wiki/JamFactory "JamFactory") ceramics and design gallery are just around the corner.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/The_Orb%2C_Adelaide_Entertainment_Centre.jpg/250px-The_Orb%2C_Adelaide_Entertainment_Centre.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Orb,_Adelaide_Entertainment_Centre.jpg)

The [Adelaide Entertainment Centre](/wiki/Adelaide_Entertainment_Centre "Adelaide Entertainment Centre"), the largest indoor sports and entertainment venue in Adelaide

The Adelaide Festival Centre (which includes the Dunstan Playhouse, Festival Theatre and Space Theatre), on the banks of the Torrens, is the focal point for much of the cultural activity in the city and home to the [State Theatre Company of South Australia](/wiki/State_Theatre_Company_of_South_Australia "State Theatre Company of South Australia"). Other live music and theatre venues include the [Adelaide Entertainment Centre](/wiki/Adelaide_Entertainment_Centre "Adelaide Entertainment Centre"); [Adelaide Oval](/wiki/Adelaide_Oval "Adelaide Oval"); [Memorial Drive Park](/wiki/Memorial_Drive_Park "Memorial Drive Park"); [Thebarton Theatre](/wiki/Thebarton_Theatre "Thebarton Theatre"); [Adelaide Town Hall](/wiki/Adelaide_Town_Hall "Adelaide Town Hall"); [Her Majesty's Theatre](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Theatre,_Adelaide "Her Majesty's Theatre, Adelaide"); [Queen's Theatre](/wiki/Queen%27s_Theatre,_Adelaide "Queen's Theatre, Adelaide"); [Holden Street Theatres](/wiki/Holden_Street_Theatres "Holden Street Theatres"); and the Hopgood Theatre.

The [Lion Arts Factory](/wiki/Lion_Arts_Factory "Lion Arts Factory"), within the Lion Arts Centre, hosts contemporary music in a wide range of genres, as does "[The Gov](/wiki/The_Gov "The Gov")" in [Hindmarsh](/wiki/Hindmarsh,_South_Australia "Hindmarsh, South Australia"). The city also has numerous smaller theatres, pubs and cabaret bars which host performances.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Thebarton_Theatre_2021.jpg/250px-Thebarton_Theatre_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thebarton_Theatre_2021.jpg)

The [Thebarton Theatre](/wiki/Thebarton_Theatre "Thebarton Theatre"), colloquially known as the "Thebby", is one of South Australia's most popular live music venues.

Further information: [Music of Adelaide](/wiki/Music_of_Adelaide "Music of Adelaide")

In 2015, it was said that there were now more live music venues per capita in Adelaide than any other capital city in the southern hemisphere,[[192]](#cite_note-199)[[193]](#cite_note-200) *[Lonely Planet](/wiki/Lonely_Planet "Lonely Planet")* labelled Adelaide "Australia's live music city",[[194]](#cite_note-201) and the city was recognised as a "[City of Music](/wiki/City_of_Music_(UNESCO) "City of Music (UNESCO)")" by the [UNESCO Creative Cities Network](/wiki/UNESCO_Creative_Cities_Network "UNESCO Creative Cities Network").[[195]](#cite_note-202) Although there were many pubs hosting live music in the CBD in past, the number has slowly diminished. The [Grace Emily](/wiki/Grace_Emily "Grace Emily") on [Waymouth Street](/wiki/Waymouth_Street "Waymouth Street"), which was refurbished as a live music venue around 2000, is popular with musicians and patrons alike.[[196]](#cite_note-203) The [Crown & Anchor](/wiki/Crown_%26_Anchor,_Adelaide "Crown & Anchor, Adelaide") ("the Cranker") was saved from demolition in 2024 after a vigorous campaign by the public as well as many musicians and politicians. New legislation passed on 11 September 2024 designates the entire Adelaide CBD as a "live music venue area", and gives protection to selected live music venues.[[197]](#cite_note-204)

In addition to its own [WOMAD](/wiki/WOMAD "WOMAD") ([WOMADelaide](/wiki/WOMADelaide "WOMADelaide")), Adelaide has attracted several touring music festivals, including [Creamfields](/wiki/Creamfields_Australia "Creamfields Australia"), [Laneway](/wiki/St_Jerome%27s_Laneway_Festival "St Jerome's Laneway Festival"), and [Groovin'](/wiki/Groovin%27_the_Moo "Groovin' the Moo") (some since defunct).

Adelaide has produced musical groups and individuals who have achieved national and international fame. These include the [Adelaide Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Adelaide_Symphony_Orchestra "Adelaide Symphony Orchestra"), the [Adelaide Youth Orchestra](/wiki/Adelaide_Youth_Orchestra "Adelaide Youth Orchestra"), rock bands [The Angels](/wiki/The_Angels_(Australian_band) "The Angels (Australian band)"), [Atlas Genius](/wiki/Atlas_Genius "Atlas Genius"), [Cold Chisel](/wiki/Cold_Chisel "Cold Chisel"), [The Superjesus](/wiki/The_Superjesus "The Superjesus"), [Wolf & Cub](/wiki/Wolf_%26_Cub "Wolf & Cub"), roots/blues group [The Audreys](/wiki/The_Audreys "The Audreys"), internationally acclaimed metal acts [I Killed The Prom Queen](/wiki/I_Killed_The_Prom_Queen "I Killed The Prom Queen") and [Double Dragon](/wiki/Double_Dragon_(band) "Double Dragon (band)"), popular Australian hip-hop outfit [Hilltop Hoods](/wiki/Hilltop_Hoods "Hilltop Hoods"), as well as pop acts like [Sia](/wiki/Sia "Sia"), [Orianthi](/wiki/Orianthi "Orianthi"), [Guy Sebastian](/wiki/Guy_Sebastian "Guy Sebastian"), and [Wes Carr](/wiki/Wes_Carr "Wes Carr"), and the internationally successful tribute act, The [Australian Pink Floyd Show](/wiki/Australian_Pink_Floyd_Show "Australian Pink Floyd Show").

Noted rocker [Jimmy Barnes](/wiki/Jimmy_Barnes "Jimmy Barnes") (formerly lead vocalist with Cold Chisel) spent most of his youth in the northern suburb of [Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth,_South_Australia "Elizabeth, South Australia"). Australian singer-songwriter [Paul Kelly](/wiki/Paul_Kelly_(Australian_musician) "Paul Kelly (Australian musician)") grew up in Adelaide and was head prefect at [Rostrevor College](/wiki/Rostrevor_College "Rostrevor College"). The first *[Australian Idol](/wiki/Australian_Idol "Australian Idol")* winner, Guy Sebastian, hails from the north-eastern suburb of [Golden Grove](/wiki/Golden_Grove,_South_Australia "Golden Grove, South Australia").[[198]](#cite_note-205)

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Radio")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of radio stations in Australia § Adelaide](/wiki/List_of_radio_stations_in_Australia#Adelaide "List of radio stations in Australia").

There are 20 radio stations that serve the metropolitan area, as well as four stations that serve only parts of the metropolitan area; six commercial stations, six community stations, six national stations and two narrowcast stations.[[201]](#cite_note-208)

[DAB+](/wiki/DAB%2B "DAB+") digital radio has been broadcasting in metropolitan Adelaide since 20 May 2009, and currently offers a choice of 41 stations all operated by the existing licensed radio broadcasters, which includes high-quality [simulcast](/wiki/Simulcast "Simulcast") of all AM and FM stations.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Sport")]

Main article: [Sport in South Australia](/wiki/Sport_in_South_Australia "Sport in South Australia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Adelaide_Oval_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Adelaide_Oval_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_Oval_-_panoramio.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Adelaide_United_FC_vs._Sydney_FC_at_Hindmarsh_Stadium_-_April_2023.JPG/250px-Adelaide_United_FC_vs._Sydney_FC_at_Hindmarsh_Stadium_-_April_2023.JPG)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_United_FC_vs._Sydney_FC_at_Hindmarsh_Stadium_-_April_2023.JPG)

[Coopers Stadium](/wiki/Hindmarsh_Stadium "Hindmarsh Stadium") hosts [Adelaide United](/wiki/Adelaide_United "Adelaide United").

The main sports played professionally in Adelaide are [Australian Rules football](/wiki/Australian_Rules_football "Australian Rules football"), soccer, [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball"), and basketball. Adelaide is the home of two [Australian Football League](/wiki/Australian_Football_League "Australian Football League") teams: the [Adelaide Football Club](/wiki/Adelaide_Football_Club "Adelaide Football Club") and [Port Adelaide Football Club](/wiki/Port_Adelaide_Football_Club "Port Adelaide Football Club"), and one [A-League](/wiki/A-League "A-League") soccer team, [Adelaide United](/wiki/Adelaide_United "Adelaide United"). A local [Australian rules football](/wiki/Australian_rules_football "Australian rules football") league, the [South Australian National Football League](/wiki/South_Australian_National_Football_League "South Australian National Football League") (SANFL), is made up of 10 teams from around Adelaide. The SANFL has been in operation since 1877 when it began as the South Australian Football Association (SAFA) before changing its name to the SANFL in 1927. The [SANFL](/wiki/South_Australian_National_Football_League "South Australian National Football League") is the oldest surviving football league of any code played in Australia.[[202]](#cite_note-209)

Until the completion of the 2012–14 renovation and upgrade of the [Adelaide Oval](/wiki/Adelaide_Oval "Adelaide Oval"), most large sporting events took place at either [Football Park](/wiki/Football_Park "Football Park") (the then home base of the [Adelaide Crows](/wiki/Adelaide_Crows "Adelaide Crows"), and the then [Port Adelaide](/wiki/Port_Adelaide_Football_Club "Port Adelaide Football Club") home game venue), or the historic [Adelaide Oval](/wiki/Adelaide_Oval "Adelaide Oval"), home of the [South Australia Redbacks](/wiki/South_Australia_cricket_team "South Australia cricket team") and the [Adelaide Strikers](/wiki/Adelaide_Strikers "Adelaide Strikers") cricket teams. Since completion of the upgrade, home games for Adelaide Crows and Port Adelaide now take place at Adelaide Oval.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Since 1884, [Adelaide Oval](/wiki/Adelaide_Oval "Adelaide Oval") has also hosted an international cricket test every summer, along with a number of [One Day International](/wiki/One_Day_International "One Day International") cricket matches. [Memorial Drive Park](/wiki/Memorial_Drive_Park "Memorial Drive Park"), adjacent to the Adelaide Oval, used to host Davis Cup and other major tennis events, including the Australian Open and the Adelaide International.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Adelaide's professional association football team, [Adelaide United](/wiki/Adelaide_United_FC "Adelaide United FC"), play in the [A-League](/wiki/A-League_Men "A-League Men"). Founded in 2003, their home ground is [Coopers Stadium](/wiki/Hindmarsh_Stadium "Hindmarsh Stadium"), which has a capacity of 16,500 and is one of the few [purpose-built soccer stadia](/wiki/Soccer-specific_stadium "Soccer-specific stadium") in Australia. Prior to United's foundation, [Adelaide City](/wiki/Adelaide_City_FC "Adelaide City FC") and [West Adelaide](/wiki/West_Adelaide_SC "West Adelaide SC") represented the city in the [National Soccer League](/wiki/National_Soccer_League "National Soccer League"). The two sides, which contest the [Adelaide derby](/wiki/Adelaide_derby "Adelaide derby") against one another, now play in the [National Premier Leagues South Australia](/wiki/National_Premier_Leagues_South_Australia "National Premier Leagues South Australia").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For two years, 1997 and 1998, Adelaide was represented in Australia's top level [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"), after the [New South Wales Rugby League](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Rugby_League "New South Wales Rugby League") had played a single game per season at the Adelaide Oval for five years starting in 1991.[[203]](#cite_note-210) The [Adelaide Rams](/wiki/Adelaide_Rams "Adelaide Rams") were formed and played in the breakaway [Super League](/wiki/Super_League_(Australia) "Super League (Australia)") (SL) competition in [1997](/wiki/1997_Super_League_(Australia)_season "1997 Super League (Australia) season") before moving to the new [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") in [1998](/wiki/1998_NRL_season "1998 NRL season"). Initially playing at the Adelaide Oval, the club moved to the more suitable Hindmarsh Stadium late in the 1998 season. As part of a peace deal with the [Australian Rugby League](/wiki/Australian_Rugby_League "Australian Rugby League") to end the [Super League war](/wiki/Super_League_war "Super League war"), the club's owners [News Limited](/wiki/News_Limited "News Limited") (who were also owners of the SL) suddenly closed the club only weeks before the start of the [1999 season](/wiki/1999_NRL_season "1999 NRL season").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Adelaide has two professional basketball teams, the men's team being the [Adelaide 36ers](/wiki/Adelaide_36ers "Adelaide 36ers") which plays in the [National Basketball League](/wiki/National_Basketball_League_(Australasia) "National Basketball League (Australasia)") (NBL) and the women's team, the [Adelaide Lightning](/wiki/Adelaide_Lightning "Adelaide Lightning") which plays in the [Women's National Basketball League](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_League "Women's National Basketball League") (WNBL). The Adelaide 36ers play at the [Adelaide Entertainment Centre](/wiki/Adelaide_Entertainment_Centre "Adelaide Entertainment Centre") while the Adelaide Lightning play at the [Adelaide Arena](/wiki/Adelaide_Arena "Adelaide Arena") (Previously Titanium Security Arena). Adelaide has a professional [netball](/wiki/Netball "Netball") team, the [Adelaide Thunderbirds](/wiki/Adelaide_Thunderbirds "Adelaide Thunderbirds"), which plays in the national netball competition, the [Super Netball](/wiki/Super_Netball "Super Netball") championship, with home games played at [Netball SA Stadium](/wiki/Netball_SA_Stadium "Netball SA Stadium"). The Thunderbirds occasionally play games or finals at the Titanium Security Arena, while international netball matches are usually played at the 10,500 seat Adelaide Entertainment Centre. The Titanium Security Arena has a capacity of 8,000 and is the largest purpose-built basketball stadium in Australia.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Tourdownunder2.jpg/250px-Tourdownunder2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tourdownunder2.jpg)

The [Tour Down Under](/wiki/Tour_Down_Under "Tour Down Under") is the first event of the [UCI World Tour](/wiki/UCI_World_Tour "UCI World Tour") calendar.

Since 1999 Adelaide and its surrounding areas have hosted the [Tour Down Under](/wiki/Tour_Down_Under "Tour Down Under") [bicycle race](/wiki/Bicycle_racing "Bicycle racing"), organised and directed by Adelaide-based [Michael Turtur](/wiki/Michael_Turtur "Michael Turtur"). Turtur won an [Olympic](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics") gold medal for Australia in the [4000 m team pursuit](/wiki/Cycling_at_the_1984_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_team_pursuit "Cycling at the 1984 Summer Olympics – Men's team pursuit") at the [1984 Los Angeles Olympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Olympics "1984 Summer Olympics"). The Tour Down Under is the largest cycling event outside Europe and was the first event outside Europe to be granted [UCI ProTour](/wiki/UCI_ProTour "UCI ProTour") status.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [2024 Women's Tour Down Under](/wiki/2024_Women%27s_Tour_Down_Under "2024 Women's Tour Down Under") cycle stage race was held in and around Adelaide, South Australia from 12 to 14 January 2024.

Adelaide maintains a franchise in the [Australian Baseball League](/wiki/Australian_Baseball_League "Australian Baseball League"), the [Adelaide Giants](/wiki/Adelaide_Giants "Adelaide Giants"). They have been playing since 2009, and their home stadium (until 2016) was [Norwood Oval](/wiki/Norwood_Oval "Norwood Oval"). From 2016 the team moved to the [Diamond Sports Stadium](/wiki/Diamond_Sports_Stadium "Diamond Sports Stadium") located near the [Adelaide International Airport](/wiki/Adelaide_International_Airport "Adelaide International Airport") due to renovations at Norwood.[[204]](#cite_note-211)

Adelaide also has an ice hockey team, [Adelaide Adrenaline](/wiki/Adelaide_Adrenaline "Adelaide Adrenaline") in the [Australian Ice Hockey League](/wiki/Australian_Ice_Hockey_League "Australian Ice Hockey League") (AIHL). It was national champions in 2009 and plays its games at the [IceArenA](/wiki/Ice_Arena_(Adelaide) "Ice Arena (Adelaide)").[[205]](#cite_note-212)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Adelaide_Street_Circuit.jpg/250px-Adelaide_Street_Circuit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_Street_Circuit.jpg)

The Adelaide Street Circuit as seen from a helicopter in November 2024. The Adelaide Oval can also be seen on the right.

The [Australian Grand Prix](/wiki/Australian_Grand_Prix "Australian Grand Prix") for World Championship [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") racing was hosted by Adelaide from [1985](/wiki/1985_Australian_Grand_Prix "1985 Australian Grand Prix") to [1995](/wiki/1995_Australian_Grand_Prix "1995 Australian Grand Prix") on the [Adelaide Street Circuit](/wiki/Adelaide_Street_Circuit "Adelaide Street Circuit") which was laid out in the city's East End as well as the eastern parklands including the [Victoria Park Racecourse](/wiki/Victoria_Park,_Adelaide "Victoria Park, Adelaide").[[206]](#cite_note-f1-move-213) The Grand Prix became a source of pride, and losing the event to Melbourne in a surprise announcement in mid-1993 left a void that has since been filled with the [Adelaide 500](/wiki/Adelaide_500 "Adelaide 500") for [V8 Supercar](/wiki/Supercars_Championship "Supercars Championship") racing, held on a modified version of the same street circuit. The Classic Adelaide, a [rally](/wiki/Rallying "Rallying") of classic sporting vehicles, is also held in the city and its surrounds.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Adelaide formerly had three horse racing venues. [Victoria Park](/wiki/Victoria_Park,_Adelaide "Victoria Park, Adelaide"), [Cheltenham Park Racecourse](/wiki/Cheltenham_Park_Racecourse "Cheltenham Park Racecourse"), both of which have now closed, and [Morphettville Racecourse](/wiki/Morphettville_Racecourse "Morphettville Racecourse") that remains the home of the [South Australian Jockey Club](/wiki/South_Australian_Jockey_Club "South Australian Jockey Club"). It also has [Globe Derby Park](/wiki/Globe_Derby_Park_(harness_racing) "Globe Derby Park (harness racing)") for [Harness racing](/wiki/Harness_racing "Harness racing") that opened in 1969, and by 1973 had become Adelaide's premier harness racing venue taking over from the [Wayville Showgrounds](/wiki/Adelaide_Showgrounds "Adelaide Showgrounds"), as well as [Greyhound Park](/wiki/Greyhound_Park "Greyhound Park") for [greyhound racing](/wiki/Greyhound_racing "Greyhound racing") that opened in 1972.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [World Solar Challenge](/wiki/World_Solar_Challenge "World Solar Challenge") race attracts teams from around the world, most of which are fielded by universities or corporations, although some are fielded by high schools. The race has a 20-years' history spanning nine races, with the inaugural event taking place in 1987. Adelaide hosted the 2012 World Bowls Championships[[207]](#cite_note-214) at Lockleys Bowling Club, becoming the third city in the world to have held the championships twice, having previously hosted the event in 1996.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Dirt track speedway](/wiki/Dirt_track_racing "Dirt track racing") is also popular in Adelaide with three operating speedways. [Adelaide Motorsport Park](/wiki/Adelaide_Motorsport_Park "Adelaide Motorsport Park"), located adjacent to the [Adelaide International Raceway](/wiki/Adelaide_International_Raceway "Adelaide International Raceway") road racing circuit at [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia,_South_Australia "Virginia, South Australia") (24 km (15 mi) north of the city centre) has been in continuous operation since 1979 after the closure of the popular [Rowley Park Speedway](/wiki/Rowley_Park_Speedway "Rowley Park Speedway"). [Gillman Speedway](/wiki/Gillman_Speedway "Gillman Speedway") located in the semi-industrial suburb of [Gillman](/wiki/Gillman,_South_Australia "Gillman, South Australia"), has been in operation since 1998 and caters to [Motorcycle speedway](/wiki/Motorcycle_speedway "Motorcycle speedway") and [Sidecars](/wiki/Sidecar_speedway "Sidecar speedway"), while the [Sidewinders Speedway](/wiki/Sidewinders_Speedway "Sidewinders Speedway") located in [Wingfield](/wiki/Wingfield,_South_Australia "Wingfield, South Australia") is also a motorcycle speedway dedicated to Under-16 riders and has been in operation since 1978.

In 2016, backed by South Australia's [Peregrine Corporation](/wiki/Peregrine_Corporation "Peregrine Corporation") opened up a multi-purpose facility; a state-of-the-art motorsporting park and a hotel alongside its newer OTR service station outside a small township of [Tailem Bend](/wiki/Tailem_Bend "Tailem Bend") currently named [The Bend Motorsport Park](/wiki/The_Bend_Motorsport_Park "The Bend Motorsport Park"). Design for thrill seekers and rev-heads the facility currently host South Australia's second Supercars motoring event during a round in August.[[208]](#cite_note-215)

Adelaide is home to the [Great Southern Slam](/wiki/Great_Southern_Slam "Great Southern Slam"), the world's largest [roller derby](/wiki/Roller_derby "Roller derby") tournament. The tournament has been held biennially over Australia's Queen's Birthday holiday weekend since 2010. In 2014, and 2016 the tournament featured 45 teams playing in two divisions. In 2018, the tournament has expanded to 48 teams competing in three divisions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transport in Adelaide](/wiki/Transport_in_Adelaide "Transport in Adelaide")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/TransAdelaideRailwayMap.svg/250px-TransAdelaideRailwayMap.svg.png)](/wiki/File:TransAdelaideRailwayMap.svg)

Adelaide's railway and tram network, served by the [Adelaide Metro](/wiki/Adelaide_Metro "Adelaide Metro")

Being centrally located on the Australian mainland, Adelaide forms a strategic transport hub for east–west and north–south routes. The city itself has a metropolitan public transport system managed by and known as the [Adelaide Metro](/wiki/Adelaide_Metro "Adelaide Metro"). The Adelaide Metro consists of a contracted bus system including the [O-Bahn Busway](/wiki/O-Bahn_Busway "O-Bahn Busway"), [7 commuter rail lines](/wiki/Railways_in_Adelaide "Railways in Adelaide") (diesel and electric), and a small tram network operating between inner suburb [Hindmarsh](/wiki/Hindmarsh,_South_Australia "Hindmarsh, South Australia"), the city centre, and seaside [Glenelg](/wiki/Glenelg_tram_line "Glenelg tram line") with more recent small offshoots in the city (to Botanical Gardens and Festival Plaza). Tramways were largely dismantled in the 1950s, but saw a revival in the 2010s with upgrades and extensions.

Road transport in Adelaide has historically been easier than many of the other Australian cities, with a well-defined city layout and wide multiple-lane roads from the beginning of its development. Adelaide was known as a "twenty-minute city", with commuters having been able to travel from metropolitan outskirts to the city proper in roughly twenty minutes. However, such arterial roads often experience traffic congestion as the city grows.[[209]](#cite_note-216)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Adelaide_O-Bahn_tunnel_passes_under_Rymill_Park_in_autumn.jpg/250px-Adelaide_O-Bahn_tunnel_passes_under_Rymill_Park_in_autumn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_O-Bahn_tunnel_passes_under_Rymill_Park_in_autumn.jpg)

The [O-Bahn Busway](/wiki/O-Bahn_Busway "O-Bahn Busway") tunnel passes under [Rymill Park](/wiki/Rymill_Park "Rymill Park") and serves the northeastern suburbs.

The Adelaide metropolitan area has one freeway and four expressways. In order of construction, they are:

* The [South Eastern Freeway](/wiki/South_Eastern_Freeway "South Eastern Freeway") (M1), connects the south-east corner of the Adelaide Plain to the Adelaide Hills and beyond to [Murray Bridge](/wiki/Murray_Bridge,_South_Australia "Murray Bridge, South Australia") and [Tailem Bend](/wiki/Tailem_Bend "Tailem Bend"), where it then continues as National Highway 1 south-east to Melbourne.
* The [Southern Expressway](/wiki/Southern_Expressway_(Australia) "Southern Expressway (Australia)") (M2), connecting the outer southern suburbs with the inner southern suburbs and the city centre. It duplicates the route of [South Road](/wiki/South_Road "South Road").
* The [North-South Motorway](/wiki/North-South_Motorway "North-South Motorway") (M2), is an ongoing major project that will become the major north–south corridor, replacing most of what is now [South Road](/wiki/South_Road "South Road"), connecting the [Southern Expressway](/wiki/Southern_Expressway_(Adelaide) "Southern Expressway (Adelaide)") and the [Northern Expressway](/wiki/Northern_Expressway "Northern Expressway") via a motorway with no traffic lights. As of 2024 the motorway's northern half is complete, connecting the Northern Expressway to Adelaide's inner north-west; the section running through Adelaide's inner west and inner south-west will begin major construction in 2025 with completion estimated for 2031.[[210]](#cite_note-217)
* The [Port River Expressway](/wiki/Port_River_Expressway "Port River Expressway") (A9), connects Port Adelaide and [Outer Harbor](/wiki/Outer_Harbor,_South_Australia "Outer Harbor, South Australia") to Port Wakefield Road at the northern "entrance" to the metropolitan area.
* The [Northern Expressway](/wiki/Northern_Expressway "Northern Expressway") (Max Fatchen Expressway) (M2), is the northern suburbs bypass route connecting the Sturt Highway (National Highway 20) via the [Gawler Bypass](/wiki/Gawler_Bypass "Gawler Bypass") to Port Wakefield Road at a point a few kilometres north of the Port River Expressway connection.
* The [Northern Connector](/wiki/Northern_Connector "Northern Connector"), completed in 2020, links the North South Motorway to the Northern Expressway.

#### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Airports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Qatar_Airways_at_Adelaide_Airport_in_2023_by_Mitch_Coad.jpg/250px-Qatar_Airways_at_Adelaide_Airport_in_2023_by_Mitch_Coad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Qatar_Airways_at_Adelaide_Airport_in_2023_by_Mitch_Coad.jpg)

A [Qatar Airways](/wiki/Qatar_Airways "Qatar Airways") plane at [Adelaide Airport](/wiki/Adelaide_Airport "Adelaide Airport") with the city skyline in the background

The Adelaide metropolitan area has two commercial airports, [Adelaide Airport](/wiki/Adelaide_Airport "Adelaide Airport") and [Parafield Airport](/wiki/Parafield_Airport "Parafield Airport"). Adelaide Airport, in Adelaide's south-western suburbs, serves almost 9 million passengers annually,[[211]](#cite_note-Adelaide_Airport-218) with an extensive number of direct flight routes within Australia, as well as other destinations in Oceania, Asia and North America. Parafield Airport, Adelaide's second airport 18 kilometres (11 miles) north of the city centre, is used for small aircraft, pilot training and recreational aviation purposes. Parafield Airport served as Adelaide's main aerodrome until the opening of the Adelaide Airport in February 1955.

Adelaide is also home to a military airport, known as [RAAF Base Edinburgh](/wiki/RAAF_Base_Edinburgh "RAAF Base Edinburgh"), located in the northern suburbs. It was built in 1955 in a joint initiative with the United Kingdom for weapon development.

### Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Health")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/North_Terrace%27s_BioMed_precinct_at_night.jpg/250px-North_Terrace%27s_BioMed_precinct_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_Terrace%27s_BioMed_precinct_at_night.jpg)

The [University of Adelaide](/wiki/University_of_Adelaide "University of Adelaide") Health and Medical Sciences Building, located in the BioMed City precinct on North Terrace

Adelaide's two largest hospitals are the [Royal Adelaide Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Adelaide_Hospital "Royal Adelaide Hospital") (RAH) in Adelaide Parklands, a [teaching hospital](/wiki/Teaching_hospital "Teaching hospital") affiliated with the University of Adelaide (800 beds), and the [Flinders Medical Centre](/wiki/Flinders_Medical_Centre "Flinders Medical Centre") (580 beds) at Bedford Park, affiliated with Flinders University. The RAH also operates additional campuses for specialist care throughout the suburbs including the Hampstead Rehabilitation Centre (150 beds) at [Northfield](/wiki/Northfield,_South_Australia "Northfield, South Australia") and the [Glenside Campus](/wiki/Glenside_Hospital_(Adelaide) "Glenside Hospital (Adelaide)") (129 beds) for acute mental health services.

Other major public hospitals are the [Women's and Children's Hospital](/wiki/Women%27s_and_Children%27s_Hospital,_Adelaide "Women's and Children's Hospital, Adelaide") (305 beds), at North Adelaide; the [Queen Elizabeth Hospital](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_Hospital,_Adelaide "Queen Elizabeth Hospital, Adelaide") (340 beds) at Woodville; [Modbury Hospital](/wiki/Modbury_Hospital "Modbury Hospital") (174 beds) at Modbury; and the [Lyell McEwin Hospital](/wiki/Lyell_McEwin_Hospital "Lyell McEwin Hospital") (198 beds) at Elizabeth Vale. Numerous private hospitals are also located throughout the city, with the largest operators being not-for-profits [Adelaide Community Healthcare Alliance](/wiki/Adelaide_Community_Healthcare_Alliance "Adelaide Community Healthcare Alliance") (three hospitals) and [Calvary Care](/wiki/Sisters_of_the_Little_Company_of_Mary "Sisters of the Little Company of Mary") (four hospitals).

In 2017, the RAH was relocated from the city's [East End](/wiki/East_End,_Adelaide "East End, Adelaide") to a new AU$2.3 billion facility built over former railyards in the West End.[[212]](#cite_note-219) The state-of-the-art hospital forms part of a new biomedical precinct called [BioMed City](/w/index.php?title=BioMed_City&action=edit&redlink=1 "BioMed City (page does not exist)") that collocates the [South Australian Health and Medical Research Institute](/wiki/South_Australian_Health_and_Medical_Research_Institute "South Australian Health and Medical Research Institute") (SAHMRI), the University of Adelaide Health and Medical Sciences building, the University of South Australia's Health Innovation Building, and the state's Dental Hospital.[[213]](#cite_note-220)[[214]](#cite_note-221) SAHMRI, with additional external funding, has built a $300 million second facility completed in 2024, which was intended to house the [Australian Bragg Centre](/w/index.php?title=Australian_Bragg_Centre_for_Proton_Therapy_and_Research&action=edit&redlink=1 "Australian Bragg Centre for Proton Therapy and Research (page does not exist)") with Australia's first [proton therapy](/wiki/Proton_therapy "Proton therapy") unit.[[215]](#cite_note-SAHMRI_2-222) Construction is underway for the [Women's and Children's Hospital](/wiki/Women%27s_and_Children%27s_Hospital "Women's and Children's Hospital") to be relocated to the precinct adjacent the RAH by 2030.[[216]](#cite_note-NWMAC-223)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Adelaide_%2825092999627%29.jpg/250px-Adelaide_%2825092999627%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adelaide_(25092999627).jpg)

The [South Australian Health and Medical Research Institute](/wiki/South_Australian_Health_and_Medical_Research_Institute "South Australian Health and Medical Research Institute") (SAHMRI), located on [North Terrace](/wiki/North_Terrace,_Adelaide "North Terrace, Adelaide")

The largest provider of community health care within Adelaide is the not-for-profit [Royal District Nursing Service](/wiki/Royal_District_Nursing_Service_(South_Australia) "Royal District Nursing Service (South Australia)"), which provides out of hospital care and hospital avoidance care.

### Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Energy")]

Adelaide's energy requirements were originally met by the [Adelaide Electric Supply Company](/wiki/Adelaide_Electric_Supply_Company "Adelaide Electric Supply Company"), which was nationalised by the [Playford](/wiki/Thomas_Playford_IV "Thomas Playford IV") government in 1946,[[217]](#cite_note-224) becoming the [Electricity Trust of South Australia](/wiki/Electricity_Trust_of_South_Australia "Electricity Trust of South Australia") (ETSA). Despite significant public opposition and the Labor party's anti-privatisation stance which left the Liberal party one vote short of the numbers needed to pass the legislation, ETSA was privatised by the [Olsen](/wiki/John_Olsen "John Olsen") Government in 1999 by way of a 200-year lease for the distribution network (ETSA Utilities, later renamed [SA Power Networks](/wiki/SA_Power_Networks "SA Power Networks")) and the outright purchase of ETSA Power[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] by the [Cheung Kong Holdings](/wiki/Cheung_Kong_Holdings "Cheung Kong Holdings") for $3.5 billion (11 times ETSA's annual earnings) after Labor MP [Trevor Crothers](/wiki/Trevor_Crothers "Trevor Crothers") resigned from the party and voted with the government.[[218]](#cite_note-225)

The electricity retail market was opened to competition in 2003 and although competition was expected to result in lower retail costs, prices increased by 23.7% in the market's first year.[[219]](#cite_note-226) In 2004, the privatisation was deemed to be a failure with consumers paying 60% more for their power and with the state government estimated to lose $3 billion in power generation net income in the first ten years of privatisation.[[220]](#cite_note-227) In 2012, the industry came under scrutiny for allegedly reducing supply by shutting down generators during periods of peak demand to force prices up. Increased media attention also revealed that in 2009 the state government had approved a 46% increase in retail prices to cover expected increases in the costs of generation while generation costs had in fact fallen 35% by 2012.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] South Australia has the highest retail price for electricity in the country.[[221]](#cite_note-228)

Privatisation led to competition from a variety of companies who now separately provide for the generation, transmission, distribution and retail sales of gas and electricity. Electricity generation comes from a range of technologies and operators. [ElectraNet](/wiki/ElectraNet "ElectraNet") operates the high-voltage electricity transmission network. [SA Power Networks](/wiki/SA_Power_Networks "SA Power Networks") distributes electricity to end users. The largest electricity and gas retailing companies are also the largest generating companies.

The largest fossil fuel power stations are the [Torrens Island Power Station](/wiki/Torrens_Island_Power_Station "Torrens Island Power Station") gas-fired plant operated by [AGL Energy](/wiki/AGL_Energy "AGL Energy") and the [Pelican Point Power Station](/wiki/Pelican_Point_Power_Station "Pelican Point Power Station") operated by [Engie](/wiki/Engie "Engie"). South Australia also has wind and solar power and connections to the national grid. [Gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") is supplied from the [Moomba](/wiki/Moomba,_South_Australia "Moomba, South Australia") Gas Processing Plant in the [Cooper Basin](/wiki/Cooper_Basin "Cooper Basin") via the [Moomba Adelaide Pipeline System](/wiki/Moomba_Adelaide_Pipeline_System "Moomba Adelaide Pipeline System")[[222]](#cite_note-EnergySA2-229) and the [SEAGas pipeline](/wiki/SEAGas_pipeline "SEAGas pipeline") from [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria,_Australia "Victoria, Australia").

In 2011, South Australia generated 18% of its electricity from [wind power](/wiki/Wind_power "Wind power"), and had 51% of the installed capacity of wind generators in Australia.[[223]](#cite_note-230)

Due to almost universal blackouts within the city during September 2016,[[224]](#cite_note-231) the state worked with [Tesla](/wiki/Tesla,_Inc. "Tesla, Inc.") to produce the world's largest electricity battery at [Hornsdale Power Reserve](/wiki/Hornsdale_Wind_Farm "Hornsdale Wind Farm") which has increased that state's electrical security to the extent in which large blackouts are no longer an event.[[225]](#cite_note-232)

### Water

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Water")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Happy_Valley_Reservoir_20070223.jpg/250px-Happy_Valley_Reservoir_20070223.jpg)](/wiki/File:Happy_Valley_Reservoir_20070223.jpg)

An aerial view of [Happy Valley Reservoir](/wiki/Happy_Valley_Reservoir "Happy Valley Reservoir"), 2007

The provision of water services is by the government-owned [SA Water](/wiki/SA_Water "SA Water"). Adelaide's water is supplied from its seven reservoirs: [Mount Bold](/wiki/Mount_Bold_Reservoir "Mount Bold Reservoir"), [Happy Valley](/wiki/Happy_Valley_Reservoir "Happy Valley Reservoir"), [Myponga](/wiki/Myponga_Reservoir "Myponga Reservoir"), [Millbrook](/wiki/Millbrook_Reservoir "Millbrook Reservoir"), [Hope Valley](/wiki/Hope_Valley_Reservoir "Hope Valley Reservoir"), [Little Para](/wiki/Little_Para_Reservoir "Little Para Reservoir") and [South Para](/wiki/South_Para_Reservoir "South Para Reservoir"). The yield from these reservoir catchments can be as little as 10% of the city's requirements (90GL per annum[[226]](#cite_note-desal-233)) in drought years and about 60% in average years. The remaining demand is met by the pumping of water from the [River Murray](/wiki/River_Murray "River Murray").[[226]](#cite_note-desal-233)

A [sea-water desalination plant](/wiki/Adelaide_Desalination_Plant "Adelaide Desalination Plant") capable of supplying 100GL per annum was built during the [2001–2009 drought](/wiki/2000s_Australian_drought "2000s Australian drought"); however, it operated at about 8% of its capacity until 2019. In December 2018, the State and Federal Governments agreed to fund a $2m study to determine how the plant could be used to reduce reliance on river water, in an effort to help save the Murray River basin and mouth (including the [Coorong](/wiki/Coorong "Coorong")) from further ecological damage.[[226]](#cite_note-desal-233)

### Communications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Communications")]

AdelaideFree WiFi is a citywide free [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi") network covering most of the inner city areas of Adelaide, primarily the [Adelaide CBD](/wiki/Adelaide_city_centre "Adelaide city centre") and Northern Adelaide precincts.[[227]](#cite_note-inone-234) It was officially launched at the Adelaide Central Markets on Tuesday 25 June 2014.[[227]](#cite_note-inone-234)[[228]](#cite_note-235)[[229]](#cite_note-236) It was originally provided by [Internode](/wiki/Internode_(ISP) "Internode (ISP)")[[230]](#cite_note-237) with infrastructure provided by outdoor [Cisco](/wiki/Cisco "Cisco") WiFi N access points attached to the top of lighting poles, as well as inside cafes and businesses across the city. In 2023, a new agreement was reached between the City of Adelaide and [TPG Telecom](/wiki/TPG_Telecom "TPG Telecom") to replace the network infrastructure, improving network speed and coverage.[[231]](#cite_note-238)

### City of Adelaide

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: City of Adelaide")]

Main article: [City of Adelaide (1864)](/wiki/City_of_Adelaide_(1864) "City of Adelaide (1864)")

The [City of Adelaide](/wiki/City_of_Adelaide "City of Adelaide"), was a 10 sail [composite](/wiki/Composite_ship "Composite ship") (timber planking on a wrought-iron frame) [clipper](/wiki/Clipper "Clipper"). It was rescued from the mooring where it sank, as the HMS Carrick, in [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") in 1991. In 2001, it was decided that the ship's name would be reverted to the City of Adelaide, formally renamed in 2013, and arrived in Port Adelaide on 3 February 2014. It was not until June 2024 that the ship was moved from the barge Bradley by Wallbridge Gilbert Aztec (WGA)[[232]](#cite_note-239) to the current home on Dock 2, as the centerpiece of a planned village in Port Adelaide's inner harbour. The ship is owned by the Clipper Ship City of Adelaide Ltd (CSCOAL).[[233]](#cite_note-240) The STV [One and All](/wiki/One_and_All "One and All"), a 42-metre two-masted square-rigged [brigantine](/wiki/Brigantine "Brigantine") and the ketch *The Failie* are moored at McLaren Wharf.[[234]](#cite_note-241)

## Sister cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Sister cities")]

The City of Adelaide has been involved in the [sister cities](/wiki/Sister_cities "Sister cities") movement since 1972. As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Adelaide&action=edit) it has long-term international partnership arrangements with five cities, known as [sister cities](/wiki/Sister_cities "Sister cities"), based on formal agreements between Adelaide and each city. This allows collaboration in the cultural, educational, business, and technical spheres. The five sister cities are:[[235]](#cite_note-sister-242)

* [![United States](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a4/Flag_of_the_United_States.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_United_States.svg.png)](/wiki/United_States "United States") [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"), United States, since 1983
* [![New Zealand](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Flag_of_New_Zealand.svg/40px-Flag_of_New_Zealand.svg.png)](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch"), New Zealand, since 1972
* [![Malaysia](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Flag_of_Malaysia.svg/40px-Flag_of_Malaysia.svg.png)](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") [George Town, Penang](/wiki/George_Town,_Penang "George Town, Penang"), Malaysia, since 1973
* [![Japan](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/9e/Flag_of_Japan.svg/40px-Flag_of_Japan.svg.png)](/wiki/Japan "Japan") [Himeji, Hyogo](/wiki/Himeji,_Hyogo "Himeji, Hyogo"), Japan, since 1982
* [![China](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Flag_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China.svg.png)](/wiki/China "China") [Qingdao, Shandong](/wiki/Qingdao,_Shandong "Qingdao, Shandong"), China, since 2013

Three cities are known as friendship cities, based on informal partnerships that promote collaboration and a friendly relationship:[[235]](#cite_note-sister-242)

* [![China](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Flag_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China.svg.png)](/wiki/China "China") [Dalian, Liaoning](/wiki/Dalian,_Liaoning "Dalian, Liaoning"), China
* [![China](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Flag_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China.svg.png)](/wiki/China "China") [Chengdu, Sichuan](/wiki/Chengdu,_Sichuan "Chengdu, Sichuan"), China
